CertificateConfig.description:
cert-manager Certificate configuration. Either set clusterIssuer for a
simple ClusterIssuer reference, or use issuer for an Issuer reference.

domains.description:
Additional DNS names beyond the ingress.tls hosts (subject
alternative names). Useful for certs that cover hostnames not yet
wired into ingress, or for wildcard + apex combos.

issuer.description:
cert-manager Issuer name (alternative to clusterIssuer). The template
uses this only when clusterIssuer is unset.

CertificateConfig.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when autoCreateCertificate is true on a deployment, or for a chart-level
Certificate not tied to one deployment.

ConfigSpec.description: One ConfigMap or Secret. type chooses the kind.

annotations.description:
Extra annotations on the ConfigMap/Secret metadata. Common use:
GitOps sync waves so configs install before workloads that consume
them, or controller-specific hints.

binaryData.description:
Pre-base64-encoded values. Use for true binary payloads (TLS certs,
binary tokens, gzip-compressed files) where re-encoding from a string
would corrupt content. Keys still must be DNS subdomain compliant.

labels.description: Extra labels on the ConfigMap/Secret metadata.

ContainerSpec.description:
A single container in a Deployment/Job/CronJob pod template.
The container name is the map key in containers.<name>.
Required: image (no registry prefix needed) and imageTag.

args.description:
Arguments override — replaces the image's CMD. Use together with
(or instead of) command. Use args alone when you only want to
override flags but keep the image's ENTRYPOINT.

command.description:
Entrypoint override — replaces the image's ENTRYPOINT.
Pass as a list of strings (exec form). The shell is NOT invoked
unless you explicitly include sh/bash as the first element.

env.description: Environment variables. Each must use exactly one of value or valueFrom.

envFrom.description: Pulls all keys from a referenced ConfigMap or Secret.

image.description:
Container image name without registry or tag. Combined at runtime
with imageTag and an optional global registry prefix.

lifecycle.description:
Container lifecycle hooks (passthrough to Kubernetes). postStart runs
immediately after the container starts; preStop runs before SIGTERM
on shutdown — useful for connection draining or LB deregistration.
The preStop sleep trick gives the service mesh time to update its
endpoint table before the container exits.

ports.description:
Named ports keyed by name. The key (e.g. 'http') becomes the
Kubernetes port name and is the identifier used by Services,
Ingresses, and Probes that reference 'port: http'.

probes.description:
Container probes. Each probe (liveness/readiness/startup) merges
with the per-deployment defaults in deploymentsGeneral.probes.

resources.description: CPU/memory requests and limits for this container.

secretRefs.description:
Names of secretRefs groups defined at the top level of values.yaml.
Each group injects multiple env vars derived from Secret keys.

securityContext.description:
Container-level security context (passthrough). Per-container settings
override pod-level ones. Use to enforce non-root, drop capabilities,
set readOnlyRootFilesystem, or pin a specific UID/GID.

volumeMounts.description: Mount entries from the deployment-level volumes list.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Forgetting imageTag — the chart requires it explicitly.
Using port-number key in ports instead of a port-name key.
ContainerSpec.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use ContainerSpec anywhere a container template is needed:
deployments, jobs, cronJobs all share this schema.

CronJobSpec.description: A single CronJob with cron-style schedule.

affinity.description: Pod affinity / anti-affinity rules for spawned Job pods (passthrough).

annotations.description:
Extra annotations on the CronJob metadata. Note: this does NOT
set annotations on the spawned Job pods — for those, use
podAnnotations.

autoCreateNetworkPolicy.description:
When true, chart renders a `kind: NetworkPolicy` named after the
cronJob. Requires a `networkPolicy:` block — strict explicit-only,
no silent allow/deny defaults. See NetworkPolicySpec.

autoCreateRbac.description:
When true, chart renders a kind: Role + kind: RoleBinding pair named
after the cronJob. Requires an `rbac:` block and a resolvable
ServiceAccount (see RbacSpec). Strict explicit-only: no silent defaults.

containers.description:
Map of containers keyed by container name. At least one is required.
The key becomes the Kubernetes container name verbatim.

labels.description:
Extra labels on the CronJob metadata. Standard chart labels are
added automatically.

networkPolicy.description:
NetworkPolicy configuration for this cronJob. Only rendered when
autoCreateNetworkPolicy is true. See $defs/NetworkPolicySpec for
full shape and validation rules.

nodeSelector.description: Node label constraints applied to spawned Job pods (passthrough).

podAnnotations.description:
Annotations on the pod template inside the CronJob's jobTemplate.
These reach the actual Job pods spawned by the controller.

rbac.description:
Namespaced RBAC configuration for this cronJob. Only rendered when
autoCreateRbac is true. See $defs/RbacSpec for full shape and
validation rules.

securityContext.description: Pod-level security context for spawned Job pods (passthrough).

tolerations.description: Pod tolerations applied to spawned Job pods (passthrough).

items.description: One toleration (passthrough).

volumes.description:
Pod volumes available to spawned Job containers (passthrough).
Often used to mount the backup target (PVC), configuration (ConfigMap),
or credentials (Secret).

items.description: One volume entry (passthrough).

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using 6-field schedule (with seconds) — Kubernetes accepts only 5 fields.
Setting timezone on Kubernetes < 1.24 — feature unsupported.

DaemonSetSpec.description: Per-instance config for a DaemonSet workload.

annotations.description:
Extra annotations merged onto the DaemonSet metadata. Use for
GitOps wave ordering, audit labels, or controller hints. Pod-level
annotations live under podAnnotations.

autoCreateNetworkPolicy.description:
When true, chart renders a `kind: NetworkPolicy` named after the
daemonSet. Requires a `networkPolicy:` block — strict explicit-only,
no silent allow/deny defaults. See NetworkPolicySpec.

autoCreateRbac.description:
When true, chart renders a kind: Role + kind: RoleBinding pair named
after the daemonSet. Requires an `rbac:` block and a resolvable
ServiceAccount (see RbacSpec). Strict explicit-only: no silent defaults.

containers.description:
Map of containers keyed by container name. At least one is
required. The key becomes the Kubernetes container name verbatim.

hostAliases.description:
Static /etc/hosts entries injected into every pod replica. Each
entry maps a single IP to one or more hostnames.

hostIPC.description: Run pods in the host's IPC namespace.

hostNetwork.description:
Run pods in the host's network namespace. Required for some
node-level agents (CNI plugins, host metrics scrapers).

hostPID.description: Run pods in the host's PID namespace.

initContainers.description:
Map of init containers keyed by name. Each uses the same shape
as a normal container. Init containers run sequentially in
alphabetical order of their keys before main containers start.

labels.description:
Extra labels merged onto the DaemonSet metadata. Standard chart
labels (app.kubernetes.io/*) are always added automatically.

minReadySeconds.description: Seconds a pod must be ready before it counts as available.

networkPolicy.description:
NetworkPolicy configuration for this daemonSet. Only rendered when
autoCreateNetworkPolicy is true. See $defs/NetworkPolicySpec for
full shape and validation rules.

nodeSelector.description:
Node label constraints for pod scheduling (passthrough). Pods only
schedule onto nodes that match all listed labels.

podAnnotations.description:
Annotations applied to the pod template (spec.template.metadata).
Common use: Prometheus scrape hints or checksum annotations that
force a rollout on ConfigMap change.

podLabels.description:
Labels applied to the pod template (spec.template.metadata).
Propagate to pods and match NetworkPolicy / Service / ServiceMonitor
selectors.

probes.description:
Default probes (livenessProbe / readinessProbe / startupProbe)
applied to every container in this DaemonSet unless the container
overrides them.

rbac.description:
Namespaced RBAC configuration for this daemonSet. Only rendered when
autoCreateRbac is true. See $defs/RbacSpec for full shape and
validation rules.

tolerations.description:
Pod tolerations for tainted nodes (passthrough). DaemonSets often
need {operator: Exists} to tolerate every taint and land on every
node.

items.description:
One toleration (passthrough). Standard fields: key, operator,
value, effect, tolerationSeconds.

topologySpreadConstraints.description:
Constraints that spread pod replicas across failure domains
(zones, nodes, racks). Rarely useful for DaemonSets, but supported
for completeness. Passthrough to Kubernetes.

items.description:
One topology spread constraint (passthrough). Required keys:
maxSkew, topologyKey, whenUnsatisfiable, labelSelector.

updateStrategy.description: Controls rollout behavior. RollingUpdate or OnDelete.

rollingUpdate.description:
Tuning knobs for the RollingUpdate strategy: maxUnavailable
(how many node pods may be unavailable during rollout) and
maxSurge (extra pods allowed above the desired count, K8s 1.25+).

volumes.description:
Pod volumes (passthrough). Each entry defines a named volume that
containers reference via volumeMounts. Host-path volumes are
common for node-level agents (log directories, host /proc).

items.description:
One volume (passthrough). Required keys: name plus one
volume source (hostPath, configMap, secret, etc.).

DeploymentDefaultsSpec.description:
Defaults shape — identical to DeploymentSpec except no required fields.
Every key here is inherited by deployments.<name> entries that omit it.
Lint rule 9 enforces key-set equivalence with DeploymentSpec.

affinity.description:
Default pod affinity / anti-affinity rules (passthrough). Use to
enforce platform-wide scheduling policy (e.g. always spread by
zone) without per-deployment configuration.

annotations.description:
Default annotations merged onto every Deployment metadata.
Per-deployment annotations override matching keys.

containers.description:
Default containers map applied when a deployment does not define
its own containers. Rarely useful — most deployments need their
own containers; prefer per-deployment definition.

dnsConfig.description:
Default pod DNS overrides applied to every deployment (passthrough).
Common use: lower `ndots` cluster-wide to reduce DNS amplification.

hostAliases.description:
Default /etc/hosts entries injected into every pod across the chart.
Per-deployment hostAliases override this entirely.

initContainers.description:
Default init containers map applied to every deployment unless
the deployment defines its own. Useful for a chart-wide setup
step (e.g. waiting for a shared dependency).

command: until nc -z vault 8200; do sleep 1; done

labels.description:
Default labels merged onto every Deployment metadata. Chart-emitted
labels (app.kubernetes.io/*) are still added automatically.

networkPolicy.description:
Default networkPolicy block applied to every deployment that sets
autoCreateNetworkPolicy: true. Per-instance networkPolicy overrides
this entirely (lists are replaced wholesale, not unioned — see
docs/how-to/network-isolated-api.md for the replace semantics).
The autoCreateNetworkPolicy flag itself is per-instance only.

nodeSelector.description:
Default node label constraints for pod scheduling (passthrough).
Per-deployment nodeSelector overrides this entirely.

podAnnotations.description:
Default annotations applied to every pod template. Common use:
Prometheus scrape hints applied to every pod by default.

podLabels.description:
Default labels applied to every pod template — propagate to running
pods and match NetworkPolicy / Service / ServiceMonitor selectors.

probes.description:
Default probes (liveness/readiness/startup) applied to containers in
every deployment unless the container overrides them. Define one
sane readiness probe here to avoid copy/paste across services.

securityContext.description:
Default pod-level security context (passthrough). Set a baseline
here (runAsNonRoot, runAsUser, fsGroup) and override only where
a specific deployment must deviate.

tolerations.description:
Default pod tolerations applied to every deployment (passthrough).
Per-deployment tolerations override this entirely.

items.description:
One toleration (passthrough). Standard fields: key, operator,
value, effect, tolerationSeconds.

topologySpreadConstraints.description:
Default topology spread constraints applied to every deployment
(passthrough). Centralize the "spread across zones" policy here
instead of repeating it on every workload.

items.description:
One topology spread constraint (passthrough). Required keys:
maxSkew, topologyKey, whenUnsatisfiable, labelSelector.

volumes.description:
Default pod volumes (passthrough). Rarely useful at the defaults
level — most volumes are deployment-specific. Available for cases
like a shared host-path log directory.

items.description: One volume entry (passthrough).

DeploymentDefaultsSpec.x-agent-when-to-use:
Set platform-wide defaults here to avoid repeating boilerplate in
every deployments.<name>.

DeploymentSpec.description:
The shape of a single deployments.<name> entry. Inherits defaults
from deploymentsGeneral (same property set, no required fields).

affinity.description:
Pod affinity / anti-affinity rules (passthrough). The chart's
`autoCreateSoftAntiAffinity` flag generates a sensible default for
host-spreading; use this field for custom rules — zone affinity,
colocation with another workload, or anti-affinity against a label.

annotations.description:
Extra annotations merged onto the Deployment metadata. Use for
GitOps wave ordering, audit labels, or controller-specific hints.
Pod-level annotations (on the pod template) live under podAnnotations.

autoCreateCertificate.description:
When true, generates a cert-manager Certificate. Requires
autoCreateIngress: true (enforced via cross-field if/then).

autoCreateIngress.description:
When true, generates an Ingress using ingress.* and the global
generic.ingressesGeneral defaults. Requires at least one container
port (enforced in _validation.tpl).

autoCreateNetworkPolicy.description:
When true, chart renders a `kind: NetworkPolicy` named after the
deployment. Requires a `networkPolicy:` block — strict explicit-only,
no silent allow/deny defaults. See NetworkPolicySpec.

autoCreateRbac.description:
When true, chart renders a kind: Role + kind: RoleBinding pair named
after the deployment. Requires an `rbac:` block and a resolvable
ServiceAccount (see RbacSpec). Strict explicit-only: no silent defaults.

autoCreateService.description: When true, generates a Service from declared container ports.

containers.description:
Map of containers keyed by container name. At least one is required.
The key becomes the Kubernetes container name verbatim.

dnsConfig.description:
Pod-level DNS overrides. Passthrough to Kubernetes `podSpec.dnsConfig`:
nameservers, searches, and options. Combined with the pod's default
dnsPolicy (usually ClusterFirst).
dnsConfig.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use to lower `ndots` for latency-sensitive services, add extra search
domains, or pin a specific upstream resolver. Most workloads do not
need this — cluster DNS defaults are usually correct.

hostAliases.description:
Static /etc/hosts entries to inject into every pod replica.
Each entry maps a single IP to one or more hostnames.
x-agent-common-mistakes: Adding hostAliases for hostnames that already resolve through cluster DNS — duplicate entries can override Services unexpectedly.
hostAliases.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when the pod must reach a host by name that is not resolvable via
cluster DNS — typically a legacy on-prem service, a cluster-external
endpoint without DNS, or a name override for testing.

hpa.description: Inline HPA configuration. Setting this also creates an HPA tied to this deployment.

initContainers.description:
Map of init containers keyed by name. Each entry uses the same shape as
a normal container (image, imageTag, env, secretRefs, etc.). Init
containers run sequentially in alphabetical order of their map keys
before the main containers start.

command: until nc -z db 5432; do sleep 1; done

x-agent-common-mistakes: Naming init containers without an ordering prefix (e.g. "01-wait", "02-migrate") when order matters — keys sort alphabetically, so unprefixed names like "migrate" and "wait" will run in unexpected order.
Using initContainers for sidecars — sidecars must live in `containers`, not `initContainers`, because init containers exit before main containers start.
initContainers.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use initContainers for one-shot setup work that must complete before the
main containers start: schema migrations, waiting on dependencies,
fetching config or secrets, fixing volume permissions.

labels.description:
Extra labels merged onto the Deployment metadata. Standard chart
labels (app.kubernetes.io/*) are always added automatically.
Pod-level labels live under podLabels.

migrations.description: Pre-deploy Job that runs database migrations before the deployment rolls out.

networkPolicy.description:
NetworkPolicy configuration for this deployment. Only rendered when
autoCreateNetworkPolicy is true. See $defs/NetworkPolicySpec for
full shape and validation rules.

nodeSelector.description:
Node label constraints for pod scheduling (passthrough). Pods only
schedule onto nodes that match all listed labels. For more expressive
constraints (anti-affinity, preferences) use affinity instead.

podAnnotations.description:
Annotations applied to the pod template (spec.template.metadata).
Common use: Prometheus scrape hints (`prometheus.io/scrape`,
`prometheus.io/port`) or a checksum annotation that forces rollout
on ConfigMap change.

podLabels.description:
Labels applied to the pod template (spec.template.metadata). These
propagate to pods and are matchable by NetworkPolicy selectors,
ServiceMonitor selectors, and Service selectors.

probes.description:
Default probes (livenessProbe / readinessProbe / startupProbe) applied
to every container in this deployment unless the container overrides
them. Define once per deployment instead of repeating on each container.

rbac.description:
Namespaced RBAC configuration for this deployment. Only rendered when
autoCreateRbac is true. See $defs/RbacSpec for full shape and
validation rules.

replicas.description: Number of pod replicas. Defaults to 1.

securityContext.description:
Pod-level security context (passthrough). Settings apply to all
containers unless a container overrides them. Common use: fsGroup
for volume permissions, runAsNonRoot/runAsUser as a baseline.

terminationGracePeriodSeconds.description:
Seconds the pod is given to shut down gracefully after SIGTERM before
the kubelet sends SIGKILL. Defaults to 30s in Kubernetes when omitted.
x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting a very large value (e.g. 3600) without a matching preStop hook or readinessProbe failure path — the rolling update will stall waiting for pods to exit.
terminationGracePeriodSeconds.x-agent-when-to-use:
Increase for workloads that need time to drain connections, flush
buffers, or finish in-flight requests (HTTP servers, queue
consumers). Decrease only for stateless workloads where a fast
recycle matters more than graceful shutdown.

tolerations.description:
Pod tolerations for tainted nodes (passthrough). Each entry tolerates
a specific taint key/value/effect — without a matching toleration, a
pod cannot schedule onto nodes carrying that taint.

items.description:
One toleration (passthrough). Standard fields: key, operator
(Equal/Exists), value, effect (NoSchedule/PreferNoSchedule/NoExecute),
tolerationSeconds (NoExecute only).

topologySpreadConstraints.description:
Constraints that spread pod replicas across failure domains (zones,
nodes, racks). Passthrough to Kubernetes `podSpec.topologySpreadConstraints`.
The labelSelector should match the workload's component labels.

items.description:
One topology spread constraint (passthrough). Required keys:
maxSkew, topologyKey, whenUnsatisfiable, labelSelector. Optional:
minDomains (K8s 1.27+), nodeAffinityPolicy, nodeTaintsPolicy.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Forgetting that labelSelector must match the deployment's pod labels — using `app.kubernetes.io/name` instead of the chart-emitted `app.kubernetes.io/component` will spread across unrelated workloads.
Using whenUnsatisfiable=DoNotSchedule on small clusters where the constraint can't be satisfied — pods stay Pending forever.
topologySpreadConstraints.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for any deployment with replicas > 1 that must survive a zone
outage. Pair `topology.kubernetes.io/zone` (DoNotSchedule) with
`kubernetes.io/hostname` (ScheduleAnyway) for both zone and host
spreading without blocking scheduling on small clusters.

volumes.description:
Pod volumes (passthrough). Each entry defines a named volume that
containers reference via volumeMounts. Supports the full K8s volume
source set: configMap, secret, persistentVolumeClaim, emptyDir,
hostPath, projected, csi, etc.

items.description:
One volume (passthrough). Required keys: name plus exactly one
volume source (configMap, secret, persistentVolumeClaim, emptyDir,
hostPath, projected, csi, ...).

DeploymentSpec.x-agent-when-to-use: Always when defining a deployment under deployments.<name>.

EnvFrom.description: Pulls every key from a ConfigMap or Secret as environment variables.
EnvFrom.x-agent-when-to-use: Use to load all keys from a ConfigMap/Secret at once.

EnvVar.description:
A single environment variable. Provide exactly one of 'value'
or 'valueFrom'.

valueFrom.description:
Source the env var from another Kubernetes object. Provide exactly
one of secretKeyRef, configMapKeyRef, or fieldRef.

configMapKeyRef.description:
Reference a single key in an existing ConfigMap. The ConfigMap
must exist in the same namespace as the pod at runtime.

fieldRef.description:
Pull a value from the pod's own object metadata (downward API):
metadata.name, metadata.namespace, status.podIP, spec.nodeName, etc.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Providing both 'value' and 'valueFrom' — only one is allowed.
Using a name that does not match POSIX env var regex.
EnvVar.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for per-container env vars. For groups of variables shared
across services, prefer secretRefs at the chart level.

HostAlias.description:
A single /etc/hosts entry injected into every pod replica. One IP
can map to one or more hostnames.

hostnames.description:
One or more hostnames that resolve to the IP. Hostnames are added
to /etc/hosts as-is; case is preserved but DNS lookups are
case-insensitive.

HostAlias.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when the pod must reach a host by name that is not resolvable via
cluster DNS — typically a legacy on-prem service or a cluster-external
endpoint without a DNS record.

HpaInlineConfig.description:
Inline HPA tied to a deployment. Setting this both enables HPA and
defines its parameters. For standalone HPAs not bound to a chart
deployment, use the top-level `hpas:` section.

behavior.description:
Scale-up/scale-down behavior tuning (passthrough to Kubernetes HPA
v2 `behavior`). Controls scaling velocity and stabilization windows.
Use to prevent flapping under noisy metrics or to slow down scale-down.

metrics.description:
One or more HPA metrics driving the autoscaler. Multiple metrics
OR: the HPA picks the recommendation that yields the highest replica
count across all metrics. At least one entry is required.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Omitting metrics — at least one is required by Kubernetes.
Setting minReplicas > maxReplicas — schema does not catch this; templates fail.
HpaInlineConfig.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when scaling decisions are driven by metrics rather than a fixed
replica count.

HpaMetric.description:
A single HPA metric. Set type and the matching subfield (resource for
cpu/memory, pods for per-pod custom metric, object for object metric,
external for external metric, containerResource for per-container).

containerResource.description:
Resource metric scoped to a single named container within the pod
(instead of summed across all containers). Useful when one container
drives load and sidecar metrics would skew the signal.

external.description:
External metric not tied to a Kubernetes object — cloud queue
depth, message broker lag, business KPI. Requires the external
metrics adapter (e.g. KEDA, custom-metrics-stackdriver).

object.description:
Metric describing a non-pod Kubernetes object (e.g. Ingress
requests-per-second on a specific Ingress). Less common than
pods/external; requires custom metrics adapter support.

pods.description:
Per-pod custom metric (averaged across all pods in the target).
Requires a custom metrics adapter (e.g. prometheus-adapter) wired
into the Kubernetes custom.metrics.k8s.io API.

metric.description:
Identifies which custom metric to read from the metrics API
by name, with an optional label selector for disambiguation.

selector.description:
Label selector applied when fetching the metric from the
custom metrics adapter. Use when the adapter exposes
multiple series under the same metric name.

resource.description:
Built-in pod resource metric (cpu or memory). The most common HPA
configuration. Prefer Utilization targets over AverageValue —
Utilization adapts to per-container requests automatically.

HpaMetric.x-agent-when-to-use: Use one entry per signal driving the autoscaler.

HpaMetricTarget.description:
HPA metric target. For Resource metrics, prefer Utilization with
averageUtilization. For Pods/Object, use AverageValue or Value.

HttpRouteBackendRef.description:
Backend Service reference. Use weight to split traffic across multiple
backendRefs (canary / blue-green).

HttpRouteConfig.description:
Gateway API HTTPRoute configuration. Shared by per-deployment
`httpRoute` and the standalone `httpRoutes:` section.

annotations.description:
Annotations on the HTTPRoute metadata. Use sparingly — Gateway API
generally prefers typed fields over annotations.

hostnames.description:
Hostnames this route matches. Each entry uses host (FQDN) or
subdomain (combined with generic.ingressesGeneral.domain). At
runtime the controller intersects these with the parent Gateway's
allowed hostnames.

labels.description: Extra labels merged onto the HTTPRoute metadata.

parentRefs.description:
Gateway objects this HTTPRoute attaches to. Required by Gateway API
— at least one. Defaults to generic.httpRoutesGeneral.parentRefs
when omitted on a per-deployment route.

HttpRouteConfig.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use Gateway API instead of Ingress when the cluster runs a Gateway
controller (Cilium, Istio, Envoy Gateway, etc.).

HttpRouteHostname.description:
Hostname for a Gateway API HTTPRoute. Provide host (FQDN) or subdomain
(which combines with generic.ingressesGeneral.domain).

HttpRouteMatch.description:
One match clause. All sub-fields are ANDed. Combine multiple matches in
the same rule to OR them.

headers.description:
Header match conditions. All listed headers must match for the rule
to apply. Standard use: route by Host (canary), by custom headers
(feature flags), or by Authorization presence.

items.description:
One header match — name+value with optional type (Exact default
or RegularExpression). Header names are case-insensitive in HTTP.

path.description:
Path match clause. type controls match semantics — PathPrefix is the
most common (any URL starting with value), Exact requires literal
equality, RegularExpression uses the implementation's regex flavor.

queryParams.description:
Query-string parameter match conditions. All listed params must
match. Useful for routing experiments or version pinning via URL.

items.description:
One query-param match — name+value with optional type (Exact
default or RegularExpression).

HttpRouteParentRef.description:
Reference to a Gateway (Gateway API). Defaults to
generic.httpRoutesGeneral.parentRefs when omitted.

HttpRouteRule.description: A single routing rule. matches selects requests; backendRefs sends them.

backendRefs.description:
Where matched traffic goes. Multiple entries split traffic by weight
(the canonical canary/blue-green setup). Without weights, traffic is
split evenly. At least one backendRef is required.

filters.description:
Inline filters applied to matching requests (request/response
mutation, redirects, mirroring, URL rewrites). Passthrough to
Gateway API — exact filter set depends on the Gateway implementation.

items.description:
One filter (passthrough to the Gateway controller). Common types:
RequestHeaderModifier, ResponseHeaderModifier, RequestRedirect,
URLRewrite, RequestMirror.

matches.description:
Request match conditions. Multiple entries are ORed: a request matches
the rule if it satisfies at least one matches[i]. Omit for a catch-all
rule that takes everything for this hostname.

timeouts.description:
Per-rule timeouts (Gateway API v1.0+). request bounds the entire
client-visible request lifetime; backendRequest bounds a single
attempt to the backend (allowing retries within request).

HttpRoutesGeneralConfig.description: Defaults for all HTTPRoutes (parentRefs, labels, annotations).

annotations.description: Default annotations merged into every HTTPRoute's metadata.

labels.description: Default labels merged into every HTTPRoute's metadata.

parentRefs.description:
Default Gateway references applied to every HTTPRoute that omits
parentRefs. Lets you centralize "all routes attach to gateway X" in
one place.

HttpRoutesGeneralConfig.x-agent-when-to-use: Use to avoid repeating the same Gateway reference on every HTTPRoute.

ImagePullSecret.description: Reference to a Secret holding registry credentials.

IngressConfig.description: Shared shape for both per-deployment ingress and standalone ingresses.

annotations.description:
Annotations on the Ingress. Common use is ingress-controller
tuning (nginx, traefik, AWS LB controller) — class-specific.
Merged with generic.ingressesGeneral.annotations.

hosts.description:
One or more hostname rules. Each entry mixes host/subdomain with a
list of path-to-backend mappings. At least one host is required.

labels.description: Extra labels merged onto the Ingress metadata.

tls.description:
TLS settings for one or more hosts. Each entry binds a secret to a
set of hosts. With autoCreateCertificate, cert-manager populates
the secret automatically.

IngressHost.description:
A hostname rule. Provide one of:
  host       — explicit FQDN (e.g. api.example.com)
  subdomain  — prefix appended to generic.ingressesGeneral.domain
  (neither) — falls back to generic.ingressesGeneral.domain as-is

paths.description:
URL path rules for this hostname. Each path routes matched
requests to a backend Service+port. At least one path is required.

IngressHost.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use subdomain in multi-environment setups where the suffix changes
per cluster (dev/stage/prod). Use host for full external pinning.

IngressPath.description: A single HTTP path rule with backend reference.

service.description:
Backend service name. Omit for per-deployment ingresses where the
backend is the deployment itself; required for standalone ingresses.

IngressPath.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use to map URL paths to backends. For per-deployment ingresses you can
omit service/portName — the deployment becomes the backend automatically.

IngressTls.description: TLS configuration for one or more hosts.

hosts.description:
List of FQDNs covered by the TLS secret. Must include every host
served over HTTPS — wildcards (e.g. *.example.com) require a
wildcard certificate in the secret.

IngressTls.x-agent-when-to-use: Use with autoCreateCertificate to wire cert-manager into the ingress.

IngressesGeneralConfig.description:
Defaults applied to every Ingress (auto-created or standalone). Annotations,
labels, ingressClassName, and tls merge with per-ingress values.

annotations.description:
Default annotations merged onto every Ingress. Per-ingress
annotations win on conflict. Use for ingress-class-wide settings
like CORS, proxy buffer size, or cert-manager wiring.

labels.description: Default labels merged onto every Ingress metadata.

tls.description:
Default TLS settings merged with per-ingress tls. Use for a
shared wildcard certificate that protects every ingress under
the global domain.

IngressesGeneralConfig.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use to set the cluster-wide ingress controller class and a global domain
so individual ingresses can use subdomain shorthands.

JobSpec.description: A single Job. Container template lives in containers.<name>.

affinity.description:
Pod affinity / anti-affinity rules for the Job's pods (passthrough).
Use when batch pods must colocate with or avoid specific workloads.

annotations.description:
Extra annotations on the Job metadata. Use for GitOps tooling
(sync waves), Helm hook ordering, or audit fields.

autoCreateNetworkPolicy.description:
When true, chart renders a `kind: NetworkPolicy` named after the
job. Requires a `networkPolicy:` block — strict explicit-only,
no silent allow/deny defaults. See NetworkPolicySpec.

autoCreateRbac.description:
When true, chart renders a kind: Role + kind: RoleBinding pair named
after the job. Requires an `rbac:` block and a resolvable
ServiceAccount (see RbacSpec). Strict explicit-only: no silent defaults.

containers.description:
Map of containers keyed by container name. At least one container
is required. The key becomes the Kubernetes container name verbatim.

labels.description:
Extra labels on the Job metadata. Standard chart labels are added
automatically. Pod-level labels live elsewhere — Jobs derive pod
labels from the controller's pod template.

networkPolicy.description:
NetworkPolicy configuration for this job. Only rendered when
autoCreateNetworkPolicy is true. See $defs/NetworkPolicySpec for
full shape and validation rules.

nodeSelector.description:
Node label constraints for the Job's pods (passthrough). Useful
for routing batch work to a dedicated nodepool or GPU node.

podAnnotations.description:
Annotations applied to the Job's pod template
(spec.template.metadata). Common use: Prometheus scrape hints if
the job exposes metrics, or a checksum to bust caching.

rbac.description:
Namespaced RBAC configuration for this job. Only rendered when
autoCreateRbac is true. See $defs/RbacSpec for full shape and
validation rules.

securityContext.description:
Pod-level security context for the Job's pods (passthrough).
Common pattern: enforce non-root for batch jobs too.

tolerations.description:
Pod tolerations applied to the Job's pods (passthrough). Required
when the Job runs on tainted/specialized nodes.

items.description: One toleration (passthrough).

volumes.description:
Pod volumes available to the Job's containers (passthrough).
Each entry defines a named volume referenced by volumeMounts on
the container.

items.description: One volume entry (passthrough).

MigrationsConfig.description:
Pre-deploy Job that runs database migrations before the matching
Deployment rolls out. When enabled, the chart emits a Helm hook
(helm.sh/hook: pre-install,pre-upgrade) Job that must complete
successfully before the Deployment is applied.

args.description:
Arguments passed to the migration container (the same container
image used by the deployment). Use this to drive the entrypoint
to its migration subcommand without changing the image.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Forgetting enabled: true — every other field is meaningless without it.
MigrationsConfig.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for schema migrations, seed data, or any one-shot setup that
must complete before the new replicaset takes traffic.

NetworkPolicySpec.description:
A `kind: NetworkPolicy` rendered from a workload's networkPolicy block.
Strict explicit-only: policyTypes must be non-empty, and each direction
listed in policyTypes requires its corresponding ingress/egress block
(use [] for explicit deny). The pod selector is derived automatically
from the workload's component label and is not user-configurable.

annotations.description:
Extra annotations merged onto the NetworkPolicy metadata. Standard
chart-emitted annotations are applied first.

egress.description:
Required when policyTypes contains 'Egress'. Empty list `[]` means
deny-all-egress. Each rule is a NetworkPolicyEgressRule.

ingress.description:
Required when policyTypes contains 'Ingress'. Empty list `[]` means
deny-all-ingress (still explicit — the user has stated intent). Each
rule is a NetworkPolicyIngressRule from networking.k8s.io/v1.

labels.description:
Extra labels merged onto the NetworkPolicy metadata. Chart-emitted
labels (app.kubernetes.io/*) are still applied automatically.

policyTypes.description:
Required, non-empty. Each value must be 'Ingress' or 'Egress'.
Determines which directions are governed by this policy. Each direction
listed here requires the corresponding ingress/egress block (use [] for
explicit deny — the chart never infers policyTypes from rule presence).

NetworkPolicySpec.x-agent-when-to-use:
Generate this block when an LLM is asked to "restrict pod traffic" or
"isolate this workload." Always list policyTypes explicitly; never
leave ingress or egress as null when its direction is in policyTypes.

PdbConfig.description: PodDisruptionBudget. Provide exactly one of minAvailable or maxUnavailable.

annotations.description: Extra annotations on the PodDisruptionBudget metadata.

labels.description:
Extra labels merged onto the PodDisruptionBudget metadata. Standard
chart labels (app.kubernetes.io/*) are added automatically.

PdbConfig.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use to keep a minimum replica count during voluntary disruptions
(node drains, upgrades).

PortSpec.description:
A container port exposed to the pod (and optionally to a Service).
The port name is the map key in containers.*.ports.

containerPort.description: TCP/UDP port the container listens on inside the pod.

servicePort.description:
External port for the auto-created Service. Defaults to containerPort
when omitted.

PortSpec.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use any time the container listens on a TCP/UDP port. Setting
servicePort here saves repeating the port when autoCreateService
is enabled.

ProbeSpec.description:
Liveness/readiness/startup probe. Mirrors the upstream PodSpec.
Common pattern is to define defaults in deploymentsGeneral.probes.

exec.description:
Exec probe — runs a command inside the container; exit code 0 = healthy.
Higher latency than httpGet/tcpSocket; use only when no network probe fits.

grpc.description:
gRPC health checking protocol (grpc.health.v1). Requires the app to
implement the standard gRPC health service. Available since Kubernetes 1.24.

httpGet.description:
HTTP GET probe against the container. Common form for web apps.
Port can be a containerPort name (e.g. "http") or an integer.

tcpSocket.description:
TCP probe — succeeds if the kubelet can open a TCP connection to
the port. Use for non-HTTP services (databases, gRPC without grpc
health, raw TCP listeners).

ProbeSpec.x-agent-when-to-use:
Define readiness for traffic gating, liveness for self-healing,
startup for slow-starting apps.

accessModes.description:
PVC access modes. ReadWriteOnce is the most common — single-node
read/write, fits all cloud block storage. ReadWriteMany requires a
filesystem that supports it (NFS, EFS, CephFS, Azure Files);
ReadOnlyMany is rarely useful in practice.

annotations.description:
Annotations on the PVC metadata. Common use: volume-related
controller hints (e.g. snapshot-class hints, backup-tool labels)
or GitOps wave ordering so PVCs bind before pods reference them.

labels.description:
Extra labels on the PVC metadata. Standard chart labels are added
automatically.

selector.description:
Label selector to bind to a specific pre-provisioned
PersistentVolume. Omit for dynamic provisioning via storageClassName
(the standard case on cloud providers).

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using 1g (lowercase) instead of 1Gi — schema requires Kubernetes unit suffix.

RbacSpec.description:
A namespaced RBAC pair (kind: Role + kind: RoleBinding) rendered from
a workload's rbac block. Strict explicit-only: rules must be non-empty,
and the workload must have a resolvable ServiceAccount (via
autoCreateServiceAccount, serviceAccountName, generic.serviceAccountName,
or a serviceAccount block). The RoleBinding always binds the chart-
rendered Role to the workload's SA in the release namespace.
ClusterRole / ClusterRoleBinding are NOT supported in v1.

annotations.description:
Extra annotations applied to BOTH the Role and RoleBinding metadata.
Standard chart-emitted annotations are applied first.

labels.description:
Extra labels applied to BOTH the Role and RoleBinding metadata.
Chart-emitted labels (app.kubernetes.io/*) are still applied automatically.

rules.description:
Required, non-empty. Each rule is a PolicyRule from rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1.
Every rule must specify non-empty verbs and at least one of resources or
nonResourceURLs. Prefer pinning resourceNames over wildcard access.

RbacSpec.x-agent-when-to-use:
Generate this block when an LLM is asked to "give this workload
Kubernetes API access" or "let this controller watch ConfigMaps."
Always pair with one of the SA-resolution paths. Keep rules to
least-privilege — pin resourceNames where you can.

ResourceRequirements.description:
Standard Kubernetes resource requests and limits. Passthrough — any
additional fields are forwarded to the container spec.

limits.description:
Maximum resources the container can use. CPU is throttled past the
limit; memory past the limit causes OOMKill. Set memory request == memory
limit to make pods Guaranteed QoS class.

requests.description:
Minimum guaranteed resources. Used by the scheduler to find a node
with enough capacity. Keys are Kubernetes resource names (cpu, memory,
ephemeral-storage, hugepages-*, plus extended resources like nvidia.com/gpu).
Values use standard Kubernetes quantity strings.

ResourceRequirements.x-agent-when-to-use: Always set; helps the scheduler and pod priority.

SecretKeyRef.description: References a single key in an existing Kubernetes Secret.
SecretKeyRef.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use to pull individual secret values into env vars or to compose
reusable secretRefs groups.

SecretRefEntry.description: A single env-var-to-Secret-key mapping inside a secretRefs group.

name.description: Environment variable name (POSIX).

ServiceAccountConfig.description:
ServiceAccount configuration for the auto-created SA. The name field
lets you reference an existing SA instead of creating a new one.

annotations.description:
Extra annotations on the ServiceAccount metadata. The most common
use is wiring up IRSA (EKS) or Workload Identity (GKE).

imagePullSecrets.description:
Per-ServiceAccount image pull secrets. Pods using this SA inherit
these secrets automatically — no need to add them to the pod template.
For chart-wide pull secrets, use generic.extraImagePullSecrets instead.

items.description: One image pull secret reference (just a name).

labels.description:
Extra labels merged onto the ServiceAccount metadata. Standard
chart labels (app.kubernetes.io/*) are added automatically.

ServiceAccountConfig.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when the deployment needs cluster-bound RBAC permissions or a
specific IAM identity (IRSA, Workload Identity).

ServiceMonitorConfig.description:
Prometheus ServiceMonitor selector and scrape settings. Inherits
label selectors and global defaults from generic.serviceMonitorGeneral.
Two modes: single-endpoint (top-level path/port/interval/scrapeTimeout/
relabelings/metricRelabelings) or multi-endpoint (endpoints[] for
heterogeneous scrape configs).

endpoints.description:
List of scrape endpoints. Use this multi-endpoint form when one
Service exposes multiple metrics ports that need different scrape
configs (e.g. main app metrics on /metrics + sidecar metrics on
/admin/metrics with a different interval).

labels.description:
Labels merged onto the ServiceMonitor metadata. The Prometheus Operator
uses these to decide which ServiceMonitors a given Prometheus instance
adopts (selector match) — typically you need at least one
well-known label like `prometheus: kube-prometheus`.

metricRelabelings.description:
Top-level metric relabelings applied to ALL endpoints (single-endpoint
mode). For per-endpoint use endpoints[i].metricRelabelings.

items.description: One metric relabel rule (passthrough).

namespaceSelector.description:
Which namespaces the ServiceMonitor should scrape. Defaults to "only
the namespace this ServiceMonitor lives in" (matchNames: [own ns]).
Set `any: true` for cluster-wide scraping (requires Prometheus RBAC).

relabelings.description:
Top-level relabelings applied to ALL endpoints (single-endpoint mode).
For per-endpoint relabelings use endpoints[i].relabelings instead.

items.description: One relabel rule (passthrough to Prometheus Operator).

x-agent-common-mistakes: Mixing top-level path/port with endpoints[] — endpoints wins, top-level is ignored.
Setting port to a containerPort that is not also a servicePort — ServiceMonitor scrapes the Service, not the Pod.
ServiceMonitorConfig.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use with autoCreateServiceMonitor: true to point Prometheus at the
auto-created Service. Use the single-endpoint form for one scrape
target; use endpoints[] when the same Service exposes multiple metrics
ports that need different scrape config.

ServiceMonitorEndpoint.description: A single scrape endpoint inside a ServiceMonitor.

metricRelabelings.description:
Prometheus `metric_relabel_configs` applied to each scraped sample
AFTER scrape. Use to drop high-cardinality metrics, rewrite labels,
or filter test-pod metrics from production aggregations.

items.description:
One metric relabel rule (passthrough). Same keys as relabelings but
operates on each metric sample, not on the target.

port.description: Named port on the Service to scrape.

relabelings.description:
Prometheus `relabel_configs` applied to the target labels BEFORE
scrape. Use to rewrite/drop the target itself: filter pods by label,
add cluster metadata, rename `__address__`.

items.description:
One relabel rule (passthrough to Prometheus Operator).
Common keys: sourceLabels, targetLabel, regex, replacement, action.

tlsConfig.description:
TLS settings for HTTPS scrape (passthrough). Use insecureSkipVerify
only for self-signed dev clusters; in prod pin caFile or use
serverName matching.

ServiceMonitorEndpoint.x-agent-when-to-use:
Define per-port scrape settings. Port must reference a named container
port that becomes a Service port (auto-created or otherwise).

ServiceMonitorGeneralConfig.description:
Defaults applied to every auto-created ServiceMonitor. Per-deployment
serviceMonitor.* overrides take precedence.

labels.description:
Default labels merged onto every auto-created ServiceMonitor.
Typically used to satisfy the Prometheus instance's serviceMonitorSelector
(e.g. `prometheus: kube-prometheus` or `release: prometheus-stack`).

namespaceSelector.description:
Default namespaceSelector for every ServiceMonitor — controls
which pod namespaces this monitor scrapes. Per-deployment values
override.

behavior.description:
Scale-up/scale-down behavior tuning (passthrough to HPA v2
`behavior`). Controls scaling velocity and stabilization windows.
Useful for slowing scale-down to avoid flapping or capping scale-up
velocity under bursty traffic.

metrics.description:
List of HPA metrics. At least one entry is required by Kubernetes.
Multiple metrics combine via max() — the HPA picks the highest
replica count across all metric recommendations.

scaleTargetRef.description:
Reference to the workload this HPA scales. Required for standalone
HPAs (inline HPAs derive it from the parent deployment). kind is
usually Deployment but can be any scalable workload (StatefulSet,
ReplicaSet, ReplicationController, custom CRDs implementing /scale).

StandaloneServiceSpec.description:
A standalone Service not tied to a chart Deployment. Useful for
external endpoints (ExternalName) or routing to pods not managed
by this chart (custom selector).

annotations.description:
Annotations on the Service metadata. Common use: cloud-provider load
balancer tuning (AWS NLB attributes, GCP load balancer class),
ExternalDNS hints, or service-mesh opt-out.

labels.description:
Extra labels on the Service metadata. Standard chart labels are
added automatically.

ports.description:
Service port mappings. At least one entry required. Each entry maps
an external port (port) to a target on the backend pod (targetPort —
integer or named container port).

selector.description:
Pod label selector that determines which pods this Service routes to.
Omit for headless Services with manually-managed Endpoints, or for
type=ExternalName.

StatefulSetSpec.description: Per-instance config for a StatefulSet workload.

annotations.description:
Extra annotations merged onto the StatefulSet metadata. Use for
GitOps wave ordering, audit labels, or controller hints. Pod-level
annotations live under podAnnotations.

autoCreateNetworkPolicy.description:
When true, chart renders a `kind: NetworkPolicy` named after the
statefulSet. Requires a `networkPolicy:` block — strict explicit-only,
no silent allow/deny defaults. See NetworkPolicySpec.

autoCreateRbac.description:
When true, chart renders a kind: Role + kind: RoleBinding pair named
after the statefulSet. Requires an `rbac:` block and a resolvable
ServiceAccount (see RbacSpec). Strict explicit-only: no silent defaults.

autoCreateService.description:
When true, the chart creates a Service for this StatefulSet.
Defaults the service to headless mode (clusterIP=None,
publishNotReadyAddresses=true). The Service is named after
serviceName, not the StatefulSet key.

containers.description:
Map of containers keyed by container name. At least one is
required. The key becomes the Kubernetes container name verbatim.

hostAliases.description:
Static /etc/hosts entries injected into every pod replica.
Each entry maps a single IP to one or more hostnames.

initContainers.description:
Map of init containers keyed by name. Each uses the same shape
as a normal container. Init containers run sequentially in
alphabetical order of their keys before main containers start.

labels.description:
Extra labels merged onto the StatefulSet metadata. Standard chart
labels (app.kubernetes.io/*) are always added automatically.

minReadySeconds.description: Seconds a pod must be ready before it counts as available.

namespace.description: Override the release namespace for this resource.

networkPolicy.description:
NetworkPolicy configuration for this statefulSet. Only rendered when
autoCreateNetworkPolicy is true. See $defs/NetworkPolicySpec for
full shape and validation rules.

nodeSelector.description:
Node label constraints for pod scheduling (passthrough). Pods only
schedule onto nodes that match all listed labels.

persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy.description:
Controls PVC lifecycle when the StatefulSet is deleted or
scaled down. Retain (default) keeps PVCs; Delete removes them.

podAnnotations.description:
Annotations applied to the pod template (spec.template.metadata).
Common use: Prometheus scrape hints or checksum annotations that
force a rollout on ConfigMap change.

podLabels.description:
Labels applied to the pod template (spec.template.metadata).
Propagate to pods and match NetworkPolicy / Service / ServiceMonitor
selectors.

podManagementPolicy.description:
OrderedReady (default) creates/deletes pods one at a time in
ordinal order. Parallel creates/deletes all pods simultaneously.

probes.description:
Default probes (livenessProbe / readinessProbe / startupProbe)
applied to every container in this StatefulSet unless the
container overrides them.

rbac.description:
Namespaced RBAC configuration for this statefulSet. Only rendered when
autoCreateRbac is true. See $defs/RbacSpec for full shape and
validation rules.

serviceHeadless.description:
When true (the chart's default when autoCreateService=true),
emit a headless Service (clusterIP=None, publishNotReadyAddresses=true).
Set to false to get a regular ClusterIP Service for the STS.

serviceName.description:
Name of the headless Service that controls the StatefulSet's
network identity. Required by Kubernetes. When
autoCreateService=true, the chart creates a Service of this
name with clusterIP=None.

tolerations.description:
Pod tolerations for tainted nodes (passthrough). Each entry
tolerates a specific taint key/value/effect.

items.description:
One toleration (passthrough). Standard fields: key, operator,
value, effect, tolerationSeconds.

topologySpreadConstraints.description:
Constraints that spread pod replicas across failure domains
(zones, nodes, racks). Passthrough to Kubernetes.

items.description:
One topology spread constraint (passthrough). Required keys:
maxSkew, topologyKey, whenUnsatisfiable, labelSelector.

updateStrategy.description: Controls rollout behavior. RollingUpdate with optional partition, or OnDelete.

rollingUpdate.description:
Tuning knobs for the RollingUpdate strategy: partition
(pods at ordinals >= partition are updated) and
maxUnavailable (K8s 1.24+, requires the feature gate).

volumeClaimTemplates.description:
PVC templates that produce a unique PVC per pod ordinal. Each
template's metadata.name is used as the volume name; mount it
via container.volumeMounts.

items.description:
One PVC template. Shape: {metadata, spec}.
`metadata.name` is REQUIRED — it becomes the volume name and the
PVC name prefix (`<name>-<sts-name>-<ordinal>`). The PVC survives
pod rescheduling and (by default) StatefulSet deletion. This is
different semantics from the `EphemeralVolumeSource` PVC template;
ignore any docs you see that say "metadata.name is server-set" —
those apply to ephemeral pod volumes, not StatefulSet templates.
x-agent-common-mistakes: Omitting metadata.name (Kubernetes rejects the StatefulSet).
Expecting the PVC to be deleted automatically — by default it is retained on STS deletion and scale-down. Use persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy to change this.

volumes.description:
Pod volumes (passthrough). Each entry defines a named volume
containers reference via volumeMounts. PVC templates from
volumeClaimTemplates are mounted by their template name, not here.

items.description:
One volume (passthrough). Required keys: name plus one
volume source (configMap, secret, persistentVolumeClaim, etc.).

StrategySpec.description:
Deployment update strategy. RollingUpdate keeps old pods alive
during rollout; Recreate kills all then starts new.

rollingUpdate.description:
Tuning knobs for RollingUpdate. maxSurge controls how many pods above
replicas can run during rollout; maxUnavailable controls how many can
go missing. Both accept integer counts or percent strings ("25%").
Ignored when type=Recreate.

StrategySpec.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use Recreate only when concurrent old+new instances cannot
coexist (e.g. exclusive lock on a resource).

VolumeMount.description:
Mounts a pod volume into the container filesystem. The name must
reference an entry in the deployment-level volumes list.
VolumeMount.x-agent-when-to-use: Use to mount ConfigMaps, Secrets, or PVCs into containers.

k8s.io.api.apps.v1.DeploymentStrategy.description:
Controls how the Deployment controller replaces old Pods with new ones
during a rollout. The `type` field is the discriminator and accepts
either `RollingUpdate` (gradual, the default) or `Recreate` (terminate
every old Pod before any new Pod starts). Only when `type: RollingUpdate`
may you populate `rollingUpdate` with `maxSurge` and `maxUnavailable`;
the chart's `StrategySpec` wrapper enforces this pairing at template
time so a stray `rollingUpdate` block with `type: Recreate` is caught
before it reaches the cluster.

rollingUpdate.description: Rolling update config params. Present only if DeploymentStrategyType = RollingUpdate.

type.description: Type of deployment. Can be "Recreate" or "RollingUpdate". Default is RollingUpdate.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `maxSurge: 0` together with `maxUnavailable: 0` — the rollout has no room to move and stalls indefinitely; at least one must be > 0.
Choosing `Recreate` for a workload that holds open connections — every Pod terminates before any new Pod is Ready, producing a full outage window equal to the startup time.
Populating `rollingUpdate` while leaving `type: Recreate` — the chart's StrategySpec rejects this, but raw Kubernetes silently ignores `rollingUpdate` in that case, masking the misconfiguration.
k8s.io.api.apps.v1.DeploymentStrategy.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use `RollingUpdate` for stateless services that can tolerate two
versions running side-by-side; use `Recreate` only for singletons or
workloads that hold an exclusive resource lock. For StatefulSet-style
workloads prefer a real StatefulSet — Deployment with `Recreate` does
not give you stable identity.

k8s.io.api.apps.v1.RollingUpdateDeployme.description:
Tuning knobs for the `RollingUpdate` Deployment strategy. `maxSurge`
caps how many Pods *above* the desired replica count the controller
may create while rolling out (extra capacity to absorb new traffic
before old Pods drain); `maxUnavailable` caps how many Pods *below*
desired may be unavailable at once. Both accept an absolute integer
or a percentage of `replicas` (e.g. `25%`). At least one must be
greater than zero — `maxSurge: 0` paired with `maxUnavailable: 0`
deadlocks the rollout. Only valid when `strategy.type: RollingUpdate`;
the chart's StrategySpec rejects this block under `Recreate`.

maxSurge.description: The maximum number of pods that can be scheduled above the desired number of pods. Value can be an absolute number (ex: 5) or a percentage of desired pods (ex: 10%). This can not be 0 if MaxUnavailable is 0. Absolute number is calculated from percentage by rounding up. Defaults to 25%. Example: when this is set to 30%, the new ReplicaSet can be scaled up immediately when the rolling update starts, such that the total number of old and new pods do not exceed 130% of desired pods. Once old pods have been killed, new ReplicaSet can be scaled up further, ensuring that total number of pods running at any time during the update is at most 130% of desired pods.

maxUnavailable.description: The maximum number of pods that can be unavailable during the update. Value can be an absolute number (ex: 5) or a percentage of desired pods (ex: 10%). Absolute number is calculated from percentage by rounding down. This can not be 0 if MaxSurge is 0. Defaults to 25%. Example: when this is set to 30%, the old ReplicaSet can be scaled down to 70% of desired pods immediately when the rolling update starts. Once new pods are ready, old ReplicaSet can be scaled down further, followed by scaling up the new ReplicaSet, ensuring that the total number of pods available at all times during the update is at least 70% of desired pods.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting both `maxSurge: 0` and `maxUnavailable: 0` — the rollout has no room to make progress and stalls forever; at least one must be > 0.
Using percentages on small replica counts without checking the rounding — `25%` of 2 replicas rounds to 1, which may not be what you wanted.
Leaving `rollingUpdate` populated while flipping `strategy.type` to `Recreate` — the chart rejects this; raw Kubernetes silently ignores the block and masks the misconfiguration.
k8s.io.api.apps.v1.RollingUpdateDeployme.x-agent-when-to-use:
Tune when default rollout speed is wrong for the workload: raise
`maxSurge` to roll forward faster on large fleets; set
`maxUnavailable: 0` for zero-downtime rollouts. For singletons or
strict capacity caps prefer `strategy.type: Recreate` over forcing
these knobs to extremes.

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.ContainerResou.description:
HPA metric source that reads CPU or memory for a single named container
inside each Pod, instead of summing across all containers as `Resource`
does. Useful when the Pod runs a heavy primary alongside a light sidecar
(Envoy, log shipper) and aggregating would smear the signal. `name`
picks the resource (`cpu` or `memory`), `container` names the target
exactly as it appears in `spec.containers[].name`, and `target` picks
the threshold style — `Utilization` (percentage of `requests`) or
`AverageValue` (absolute per-Pod value).

container.description: container is the name of the container in the pods of the scaling target

name.description: name is the name of the resource in question.

target.description: target specifies the target value for the given metric

x-agent-common-mistakes: Typo in `container:` not matching any name in `spec.containers[].name` — the metric source becomes unresolvable, HPA reports `<unknown>`, and scaling stalls.
Using `type: Utilization` while the target container has no CPU/memory `requests` — utilization is `usage/requests`, with no denominator the ratio is undefined.
Choosing ContainerResource for a single-container Pod — adds boilerplate without benefit; use plain `Resource` instead.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.ContainerResou.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when the Pod has multiple containers and scaling should react only
to the main workload — sidecars like Envoy or fluent-bit otherwise
inflate the average and either trigger scale-up on idle pods or hide
real load behind sidecar capacity. For single-container Pods stick
with `Resource` for simpler config.

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.CrossVersionOb.description:
Reference to another Kubernetes object by `apiVersion`, `kind`, and
`name`. Inside an HPA spec it picks the scale target (typically the
Deployment the HPA scales); inside an `Object` metric source it
identifies which object's metric to read. The chart wires
`scaleTargetRef` automatically from the parent Deployment name when
you declare `hpa:` under a deployment — you only need to supply this
reference manually for `Object` metric sources that point at a
different object (e.g. an Ingress for RPS).

apiVersion.description: apiVersion is the API version of the referent

kind.description: kind is the kind of the referent; More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#types-kinds

name.description: name is the name of the referent; More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names/#names

x-agent-common-mistakes: Omitting `apiVersion` and relying on a default — the field is required by the API and the HPA spec is rejected at admission.
Pointing at an object in a different namespace — the reference is namespace-local; the metric lookup silently fails.
Using `kind: Deployment` here for the scale target manually when the chart already wires it — duplicates and can drift from the actual workload name.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.CrossVersionOb.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use inside an `Object` metric source to point at the object carrying
the metric (Ingress for RPS, Service for connection count). You do
not need this for `scaleTargetRef` — the chart populates it from
the Deployment context.

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.ExternalMetric.description:
HPA metric source for signals that live outside the cluster — queue
depth, cloud-provider metrics, third-party APIs. The metric is
identified by `metric.name` plus an optional `metric.selector`
(label-matched against the metric's dimensions in the external
adapter). `target` picks the threshold style — typically
`AverageValue` (the external value divided by current replica count
defines the target per Pod) or `Value` (absolute, ignoring replica
count). Requires a cluster-installed external-metrics adapter
(KEDA, cloud-provider metrics adapter); the bare cluster cannot
resolve external metrics.

metric.description: metric identifies the target metric by name and selector

target.description: target specifies the target value for the given metric

x-agent-common-mistakes: Forgetting to install the external-metrics adapter — HPA shows `FailedGetExternalMetric` and scaling does not react.
Confusing `Value` and `AverageValue` — `Value` is absolute, `AverageValue` is divided by current replica count; the wrong choice produces order-of-magnitude scaling errors.
Setting `target.averageValue` as an integer rather than a quoted string when the value needs a non-default unit (e.g. `"500m"`) — YAML parses some values silently differently than expected.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.ExternalMetric.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for autoscaling on signals that aren't pod or k8s-object metrics
— queue depth, request rate from a CDN, ML model load. Combines well
with KEDA, which exposes a wide catalog of external metrics. For
cluster-internal signals prefer `Pods` (per-Pod) or `Object`
(k8s-object) metrics.

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.HPAScalingPoli.description:
One rate-limit rule inside an HPAScalingRules block. `type` picks the
unit — `Pods` (absolute number of pods) or `Percent` (percent of
current replicas); `value` is the magnitude; `periodSeconds` is the
sliding window the policy applies over. Within an HPAScalingRules
block multiple policies are evaluated and the selectPolicy
(`Max`/`Min`/`Disabled`) picks which one wins for that direction.

periodSeconds.description: periodSeconds specifies the window of time for which the policy should hold true. PeriodSeconds must be greater than zero and less than or equal to 1800 (30 min).

type.description: type is used to specify the scaling policy.

value.description: value contains the amount of change which is permitted by the policy. It must be greater than zero

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `periodSeconds` longer than the HPA sync period (15s by default) without realizing the policy is evaluated only at sync ticks — short windows under 15s are effectively rounded up.
Using `type: Percent` on a 1-replica Deployment — `Percent` of 1 with `value: 100` permits +1 pod per period, which is fine, but `value: 50` rounds to 0 and effectively disables scale-up.

Defining a single policy and assuming `selectPolicy` doesn't matter — the default is `Max`, which picks the more aggressive policy across multiple entries; on `scaleDown` you usually want `Min` instead.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.HPAScalingPoli.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use `Percent` to cap scaling proportional to fleet size (good for
large deployments where doubling fast is fine but +1000 is not). Use
`Pods` to cap the absolute step size (good for small fleets where a
percent of 2 rounds awkwardly). Combine multiple policies inside one
HPAScalingRules and rely on `selectPolicy: Min` for conservative
behavior.

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.HPAScalingRule.description:
Per-direction (scale-up or scale-down) rate-limit configuration.
`stabilizationWindowSeconds` is the lookback window — for scale-down
the controller takes the highest desired-replica count seen in the
window (slowing aggressive drops); the default is 300s for scale-down
and 0s for scale-up. `policies` lists HPAScalingPolicy entries that
cap step magnitude; `selectPolicy` (`Max`, `Min`, `Disabled`) picks
which policy applies when multiple match. `Disabled` freezes scaling
in that direction entirely — useful as a kill switch for scale-down
during incidents.

policies.description: policies is a list of potential scaling polices which can be used during scaling. If not set, use the default values: - For scale up: allow doubling the number of pods, or an absolute change of 4 pods in a 15s window. - For scale down: allow all pods to be removed in a 15s window.

selectPolicy.description: selectPolicy is used to specify which policy should be used. If not set, the default value Max is used.

stabilizationWindowSeconds.description: stabilizationWindowSeconds is the number of seconds for which past recommendations should be considered while scaling up or scaling down. StabilizationWindowSeconds must be greater than or equal to zero and less than or equal to 3600 (one hour). If not set, use the default values: - For scale up: 0 (i.e. no stabilization is done). - For scale down: 300 (i.e. the stabilization window is 300 seconds long).

tolerance.description:
tolerance is the tolerance on the ratio between the current and desired metric value under which no updates are made to the desired number of replicas (e.g. 0.01 for 1%). Must be greater than or equal to zero. If not set, the default cluster-wide tolerance is applied (by default 10%).

For example, if autoscaling is configured with a memory consumption target of 100Mi, and scale-down and scale-up tolerances of 5% and 1% respectively, scaling will be triggered when the actual consumption falls below 95Mi or exceeds 101Mi.

This is an beta field and requires the HPAConfigurableTolerance feature gate to be enabled.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting a tiny `stabilizationWindowSeconds` on `scaleDown` (e.g. 30) — pods flap as load oscillates; the default 300s exists for good reason.

Combining `selectPolicy: Max` with conservative-looking policies and being surprised by rapid scaling — `Max` picks the *most* aggressive applicable policy; switch to `Min` if you wanted the conservative one to bind.
Forgetting `policies` is required — an empty HPAScalingRules block is rejected by the API.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.HPAScalingRule.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use under `behavior.scaleDown` to slow drops and avoid flapping
(longer stabilization window + `selectPolicy: Min`). Use under
`behavior.scaleUp` to allow fast bursts (`selectPolicy: Max`,
short window). Set `selectPolicy: Disabled` as an explicit "do not
scale this direction" — useful during planned events.

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.HorizontalPodA.description:
Wrapper that bundles separate `scaleUp` and `scaleDown` HPAScalingRules
blocks. Sits under `hpa.behavior` and lets you shape each direction
independently — typically aggressive `scaleUp` to absorb traffic spikes
and conservative `scaleDown` (long stabilization window, `selectPolicy:
Min`) to avoid pod-flapping. Omit a direction's block to use the API
defaults (0s window + 100% step for scale-up; 300s window + 100% step
for scale-down).

scaleDown.description: scaleDown is scaling policy for scaling Down. If not set, the default value is to allow to scale down to minReplicas pods, with a 300 second stabilization window (i.e., the highest recommendation for the last 300sec is used).

scaleUp.description:
scaleUp is scaling policy for scaling Up. If not set, the default value is the higher of:
  * increase no more than 4 pods per 60 seconds
  * double the number of pods per 60 seconds
No stabilization is used.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting only one direction (e.g. only `scaleUp`) and assuming the other inherits — it does, but only from API defaults, which may not match your intent.
Using `scaleDown: { selectPolicy: Disabled }` permanently — replicas can only grow, eventually consuming budget; this should be a temporary kill switch, not the steady state.
Putting policies directly under `behavior` — the API expects `scaleUp` / `scaleDown` wrappers; flat layouts are rejected.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.HorizontalPodA.x-agent-when-to-use:
Set this whenever you care about *how* the HPA reacts, not just *when*.
The default scale-up is already fast; tuning `scaleDown` is usually
where teams add value — a longer stabilization window prevents the
"scale up, scale down, scale up" cycle around midday/evening traffic
transitions.

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.MetricIdentifi.description:
Name + optional `selector` pair that picks out one metric from the
metrics adapter. The `name` is the metric's identifier as exposed by
the adapter (the custom-metrics or external-metrics API); `selector` is
a `LabelSelector` against the metric's labels and is needed only when
the same metric name exists with multiple dimensions (e.g. one
`queue_depth` per queue name).

name.description: name is the name of the given metric

selector.description: selector is the string-encoded form of a standard kubernetes label selector for the given metric When set, it is passed as an additional parameter to the metrics server for more specific metrics scoping. When unset, just the metricName will be used to gather metrics.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Misspelling `name:` — the metric is `<unknown>` and the HPA never reacts; the typo is silent at admission time.
Using a `selector` with labels the metrics adapter doesn't expose on this metric — match returns zero series, the metric is unresolvable.
Forgetting that some adapters require the metric name to be lowercase/normalized — e.g. Prometheus adapter strips characters and rewrites names by rule.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.MetricIdentifi.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use a bare `name` when the metric is unique. Add a `selector` only
when you need to disambiguate which dimensional slice of the metric
to read — for example, picking one queue out of many.

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.MetricSpec.description:
One entry in the HPA's `metrics[]` list, used by the chart inside
`hpa.metrics`. The `type` field is the discriminator and must match the
populated sibling block: `Resource` → `resource`, `ContainerResource` →
`containerResource`, `Pods` → `pods`, `Object` → `object`, `External`
→ `external`. Multiple MetricSpec entries are evaluated independently
and the HPA picks the highest desired replica count across them, so
adding a metric never lowers the floor — it can only raise the ceiling.

containerResource.description: containerResource refers to a resource metric (such as those specified in requests and limits) known to Kubernetes describing a single container in each pod of the current scale target (e.g. CPU or memory). Such metrics are built in to Kubernetes, and have special scaling options on top of those available to normal per-pod metrics using the "pods" source.

external.description: external refers to a global metric that is not associated with any Kubernetes object. It allows autoscaling based on information coming from components running outside of cluster (for example length of queue in cloud messaging service, or QPS from loadbalancer running outside of cluster).

object.description: object refers to a metric describing a single kubernetes object (for example, hits-per-second on an Ingress object).

pods.description: pods refers to a metric describing each pod in the current scale target (for example, transactions-processed-per-second).  The values will be averaged together before being compared to the target value.

resource.description: resource refers to a resource metric (such as those specified in requests and limits) known to Kubernetes describing each pod in the current scale target (e.g. CPU or memory). Such metrics are built in to Kubernetes, and have special scaling options on top of those available to normal per-pod metrics using the "pods" source.

type.description: type is the type of metric source.  It should be one of "ContainerResource", "External", "Object", "Pods" or "Resource", each mapping to a matching field in the object.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `type: Resource` but omitting the `resource:` block (or filling `pods:` instead) — the HPA controller logs a validation error and the autoscaler stops reconciling that target.

Mixing the legacy v1 `targetCPUUtilizationPercentage` shorthand with a v2 `metrics[]` list — the controller honors `metrics[]` and silently ignores the shorthand, which often looks like the threshold is wrong.

Using `target.type: Utilization` against a container without a CPU `requests` value — utilization is computed as usage/request, so missing requests make the ratio undefined and the HPA reports `<unknown>`.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.MetricSpec.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use `Resource` for CPU/memory utilization (the common case); use
`Pods` for application-reported per-Pod metrics; use `Object` when the
signal lives on another Kubernetes object (e.g. Ingress RPS); use
`External` for off-cluster signals (queue depth, cloud metrics).
Prefer the v2 autoscaling API over the legacy v1 `targetCPUUtilization`
single-metric shortcut.

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.MetricTarget.description:
Threshold spec for an HPA metric source. `type` is the discriminator
and selects exactly one of `value` (absolute total), `averageValue`
(per-Pod total ÷ current replicas), or `averageUtilization` (percent
of `requests`, valid only for Resource/ContainerResource sources).
The HPA's desired replica count is computed so the observed metric
moves toward this target; the choice between `Value` and
`AverageValue` is the most common source of order-of-magnitude
scaling bugs.

averageUtilization.description: averageUtilization is the target value of the average of the resource metric across all relevant pods, represented as a percentage of the requested value of the resource for the pods. Currently only valid for Resource metric source type

averageValue.description: averageValue is the target value of the average of the metric across all relevant pods (as a quantity)

type.description: type represents whether the metric type is Utilization, Value, or AverageValue

value.description: value is the target value of the metric (as a quantity).

x-agent-common-mistakes: Mixing `type: Utilization` with a non-Resource metric source — utilization is only defined for built-in CPU/memory metrics; the API rejects it elsewhere.
Setting `averageUtilization: 70` without `requests` on the target container — utilization is `usage/requests`; with no denominator the HPA reports `<unknown>`.

Choosing `Value` when you meant `AverageValue` — `Value` is the total across all replicas; on a 10-replica fleet with `Value: 1000` the HPA tries to keep total at 1000, so each pod sees only ~100, which is rarely what you intended.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.MetricTarget.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use `Utilization` for CPU/memory resource metrics (the common case)
to express targets as % of requests. Use `AverageValue` when the
metric is a per-Pod count (queue messages, RPS-per-Pod). Use plain
`Value` only when the metric is intrinsically singular (a total
cluster-wide rate that the HPA should divide across replicas).

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.ObjectMetricSo.description:
HPA metric source bound to a metric on a specific Kubernetes object —
typically RPS on an Ingress or connections-per-second on a Service.
`describedObject` (a CrossVersionObjectReference) points at the
object; `metric` (MetricIdentifier) names the metric; `target` is a
MetricTarget. Unlike `Pods`, the metric is read once for the object
(not averaged across replicas), so `target.type: Value` is the usual
choice and the HPA divides the target across the current replica
count internally.

describedObject.description: describedObject specifies the descriptions of a object,such as kind,name apiVersion

metric.description: metric identifies the target metric by name and selector

target.description: target specifies the target value for the given metric

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `target.type: Utilization` — only valid for Resource/ContainerResource sources; the API rejects it here.
Pointing `describedObject` at an object in a different namespace — the reference is namespace-local and the metric lookup silently fails.
Using `AverageValue` for a metric the adapter exposes only as a total — the math the HPA does (target × replicas vs raw value) ends up off by a factor of replica count.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.ObjectMetricSo.x-agent-when-to-use:
