Use when the signal lives on a k8s object (Ingress, Service,
GatewayAPI HTTPRoute) rather than on the Pods themselves. RPS
autoscaling against an Ingress is the canonical case — load
balancer metrics naturally aggregate at the Ingress, not the Pod.

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.PodsMetricSour.description:
HPA metric source for per-Pod application metrics — anything an app
reports about itself, like in-flight requests, queue depth observed
per replica, or active goroutines. The metric (named by
MetricIdentifier) is read per matching Pod via the custom-metrics
API and averaged; `target.type` must be `AverageValue` (the only
value the API accepts here). Requires a custom-metrics adapter
(commonly the Prometheus adapter) running in the cluster.

metric.description: metric identifies the target metric by name and selector

target.description: target specifies the target value for the given metric

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `target.type` to anything other than `AverageValue` — the API rejects `Value` and `Utilization` for Pods metric sources.
Forgetting to install/configure the custom-metrics adapter — HPA reports `FailedGetPodsMetric` and never scales.

Naming the metric inconsistently with the adapter's exposed name — e.g. Prometheus adapter rewrites `http_requests_total` per its rules; the HPA name must match the *exposed* name, not the raw Prometheus name.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.PodsMetricSour.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when the autoscaling signal is a per-Pod application metric
reported via /metrics or similar. Pair with the Prometheus adapter
to expose Prometheus metrics through the custom-metrics API. For
built-in CPU/memory prefer `Resource`; for k8s-object metrics
prefer `Object`; for off-cluster signals prefer `External`.

k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.ResourceMetric.description:
The default HPA metric source for built-in Pod resources. `name`
picks `cpu` or `memory`; the value is *summed across all containers
in the Pod* and then averaged across Pods — this is the distinguishing
behavior from ContainerResource, which targets a single named
container. `target` is a MetricTarget; `Utilization` is the canonical
choice (percent of `requests`) but `AverageValue` is supported for
absolute thresholds. Requires the metrics-server (or an equivalent
metrics.k8s.io provider) running in the cluster.

name.description: name is the name of the resource in question.

target.description: target specifies the target value for the given metric

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using `type: Utilization` while the target container has no CPU/memory `requests` — utilization is `usage/requests`; with no denominator the HPA shows `<unknown>` and never reacts.
Picking `memory` without realizing Java/JVM apps rarely release heap — RSS plateaus and HPA never scales down; CPU is usually the better resource metric for JVM workloads.
Setting `averageUtilization` over 100 — technically allowed (lets pods exceed requests on average), but a value over ~90 leaves no headroom for traffic spikes between HPA reconciliations.
k8s.io.api.autoscaling.v2.ResourceMetric.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use this by default for CPU/memory autoscaling on single-container
workloads. For multi-container Pods where sidecar usage would
pollute the signal, switch to `ContainerResource`. For non-CPU/non-
memory signals, use `Pods` / `Object` / `External`.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.AWSElasticBlockStoreV.description:
Volume backed by an existing AWS EBS volume. Legacy in-tree driver; for
new workloads prefer the CSI ebs.csi.aws.com driver.

fsType.description: fsType is the filesystem type of the volume that you want to mount. Tip: Ensure that the filesystem type is supported by the host operating system. Examples: "ext4", "xfs", "ntfs". Implicitly inferred to be "ext4" if unspecified. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#awselasticblockstore

partition.description: partition is the partition in the volume that you want to mount. If omitted, the default is to mount by volume name. Examples: For volume /dev/sda1, you specify the partition as "1". Similarly, the volume partition for /dev/sda is "0" (or you can leave the property empty).

readOnly.description: readOnly value true will force the readOnly setting in VolumeMounts. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#awselasticblockstore

volumeID.description: volumeID is unique ID of the persistent disk resource in AWS (Amazon EBS volume). More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#awselasticblockstore

k8s.io.api.core.v1.Affinity.description:
Kubernetes Affinity combines three independent rule sets — nodeAffinity,
podAffinity, and podAntiAffinity — that influence where the scheduler
can place pods. Use it for anything beyond simple key=value node label
matching (which `nodeSelector` covers more concisely). The chart auto-
injects soft pod anti-affinity per topology zone when
`autoCreateSoftAntiAffinity: true`; any podAntiAffinity you define
here wins over the auto-injected rule (merge semantics — your rules
are appended).

nodeAffinity.description: Describes node affinity scheduling rules for the pod.

podAffinity.description: Describes pod affinity scheduling rules (e.g. co-locate this pod in the same node, zone, etc. as some other pod(s)).

podAntiAffinity.description: Describes pod anti-affinity scheduling rules (e.g. avoid putting this pod in the same node, zone, etc. as some other pod(s)).

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` for soft preferences — that variant is HARD, pods stay Pending forever if no node matches. Use `preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` with a weight for soft.
Mixing nodeAffinity and nodeSelector for the same constraint — they AND together at scheduling time; pods need to satisfy both, which is rarely what you meant.
Forgetting `topologyKey` in podAffinityTerm — it has no default; the scheduler errors at admission time.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.Affinity.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for scheduling preferences beyond key=value node-label matching.
Simple "place pod on node with label X" → prefer `nodeSelector`. Spread
pods across zones → prefer `topologySpreadConstraints` (modern, more
predictable than weighted podAntiAffinity).

k8s.io.api.core.v1.AppArmorProfile.description:
AppArmor security profile applied to the container's processes
(Linux nodes only — most commonly Ubuntu/Debian; not available on
RHEL/Fedora which use SELinux instead). `type` is `RuntimeDefault`
(the container runtime's default profile), `Unconfined` (no
AppArmor mediation), or `Localhost` (a named profile pre-loaded
onto the node, identified by `localhostProfile: <profile-name>`).
Kubernetes 1.30+ uses this typed field; older clusters used the
`container.apparmor.security.beta.kubernetes.io/<container>`
annotation pattern which still works but is deprecated.

localhostProfile.description: localhostProfile indicates a profile loaded on the node that should be used. The profile must be preconfigured on the node to work. Must match the loaded name of the profile. Must be set if and only if type is "Localhost".

type.description:
type indicates which kind of AppArmor profile will be applied. Valid options are:
  Localhost - a profile pre-loaded on the node.
  RuntimeDefault - the container runtime's default profile.
  Unconfined - no AppArmor enforcement.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `type: Localhost` with a `localhostProfile` name that isn't loaded on every node — Pod fails on some nodes and works on others depending on scheduling.
Mixing the annotation-style API (`container.apparmor.security.beta...`) with the typed field — they conflict; pick one and stick to it (the typed field on 1.30+).
Expecting AppArmor to be active on SELinux distros — it isn't; the field is silently ignored on RHEL/CentOS/Fedora-derived nodes.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.AppArmorProfile.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use `RuntimeDefault` on AppArmor-enabled distros for a sensible
baseline. Use `Localhost` only when you've loaded a custom profile
via DaemonSet or node provisioning. Don't bother setting it on
SELinux-only nodes (RHEL family) — AppArmor isn't active there
and the field becomes a no-op.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.AzureDiskVolumeSource.description:
Volume backed by an existing Azure managed or unmanaged disk. Legacy
in-tree driver; prefer the disk.csi.azure.com CSI driver for new work.

cachingMode.description: cachingMode is the Host Caching mode: None, Read Only, Read Write.

diskName.description: diskName is the Name of the data disk in the blob storage

diskURI.description: diskURI is the URI of data disk in the blob storage

fsType.description: fsType is Filesystem type to mount. Must be a filesystem type supported by the host operating system. Ex. "ext4", "xfs", "ntfs". Implicitly inferred to be "ext4" if unspecified.

kind.description: kind expected values are Shared: multiple blob disks per storage account  Dedicated: single blob disk per storage account  Managed: azure managed data disk (only in managed availability set). defaults to shared

readOnly.description: readOnly Defaults to false (read/write). ReadOnly here will force the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.AzureFileVolumeSource.description:
Volume backed by an Azure Files share, mounted using a referenced Secret
with the storage account name and key.

readOnly.description: readOnly defaults to false (read/write). ReadOnly here will force the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts.

secretName.description: secretName is the  name of secret that contains Azure Storage Account Name and Key

shareName.description: shareName is the azure share Name

k8s.io.api.core.v1.CSIVolumeSource.description:
Inline CSI volume — the kubelet asks a CSI driver to mount a volume
for this Pod directly, without going through a PVC. `driver` is the
CSI driver name (e.g. `secrets-store.csi.k8s.io`,
`disk.csi.azure.com`); `volumeAttributes` is a string map passed to
the driver's mount call (driver-specific keys); `nodePublishSecretRef`
points to a Secret with credentials the driver needs. Inline CSI is
most commonly used for *ephemeral* CSI volumes (the
secrets-store-csi-driver pattern); for durable storage prefer PVCs.
Not all CSI drivers support inline volumes — check the driver's
`Mode` field (`Ephemeral` or `Persistent`).

driver.description: driver is the name of the CSI driver that handles this volume. Consult with your admin for the correct name as registered in the cluster.

fsType.description: fsType to mount. Ex. "ext4", "xfs", "ntfs". If not provided, the empty value is passed to the associated CSI driver which will determine the default filesystem to apply.

nodePublishSecretRef.description: nodePublishSecretRef is a reference to the secret object containing sensitive information to pass to the CSI driver to complete the CSI NodePublishVolume and NodeUnpublishVolume calls. This field is optional, and  may be empty if no secret is required. If the secret object contains more than one secret, all secret references are passed.

readOnly.description: readOnly specifies a read-only configuration for the volume. Defaults to false (read/write).

volumeAttributes.description: volumeAttributes stores driver-specific properties that are passed to the CSI driver. Consult your driver's documentation for supported values.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using inline CSI with a driver that doesn't support ephemeral mode — the kubelet rejects the volume at Pod start; check the driver's CSIDriver resource for `Ephemeral` in `volumeLifecycleModes`.
Putting credential material directly in `volumeAttributes` — it's stored in plain text on the API server; use `nodePublishSecretRef` for sensitive material.
Trying to mount a multi-Pod shared CSI volume inline — inline CSI is per-Pod; for shared access use a PVC bound to a CSI driver that supports `ReadWriteMany`.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.CSIVolumeSource.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for ephemeral CSI volumes — most prominently
secrets-store-csi-driver mounts that bring cloud-provider secrets
into the Pod filesystem. For durable storage prefer a PVC pointing
at a CSI-backed StorageClass.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.Capabilities.description:
Linux kernel capabilities the container gets in addition to (or
removed from) the runtime's default capability set. `add[]` grants
caps; `drop[]` removes them; both accept canonical names without the
`CAP_` prefix (e.g. `NET_BIND_SERVICE`, not `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE`).
The Pod Security Standard `restricted` profile requires `drop:
[ALL]` and forbids any `add` other than `NET_BIND_SERVICE`. Only
valid inside container `securityContext`, not Pod-level
`securityContext`. The `ALL` token in `drop` is the runtime's full
set; combining `drop: [ALL]` with explicit `add` is the standard
least-privilege pattern.
x-agent-common-mistakes: Using only `add` without `drop: [ALL]` — you keep the runtime's full default set and merely add to it, defeating least-privilege; almost always not what was intended.
Including the `CAP_` prefix in capability names (`CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE`) — Kubernetes rejects this format; names must be unprefixed.

Granting capabilities like `SYS_ADMIN` instead of figuring out which specific capability is missing — `SYS_ADMIN` is broad enough that workloads often pass with it but the result is effectively as privileged as root.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.Capabilities.x-agent-when-to-use:
Always pair `drop: [ALL]` with whatever capabilities the workload
actually needs (most need none). The most common single
capability is `NET_BIND_SERVICE` for binding to ports below 1024
without root. For workloads that don't bind privileged ports,
`drop: [ALL]` alone meets `restricted` PSS.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.CephFSVolumeSource.description:
Volume backed by a CephFS mount. Lists monitors, the path, user, and an
authentication secret or keyring file.

monitors.description: monitors is Required: Monitors is a collection of Ceph monitors More info: https://examples.k8s.io/volumes/cephfs/README.md#how-to-use-it

path.description: path is Optional: Used as the mounted root, rather than the full Ceph tree, default is /

readOnly.description: readOnly is Optional: Defaults to false (read/write). ReadOnly here will force the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts. More info: https://examples.k8s.io/volumes/cephfs/README.md#how-to-use-it

secretFile.description: secretFile is Optional: SecretFile is the path to key ring for User, default is /etc/ceph/user.secret More info: https://examples.k8s.io/volumes/cephfs/README.md#how-to-use-it

secretRef.description: secretRef is Optional: SecretRef is reference to the authentication secret for User, default is empty. More info: https://examples.k8s.io/volumes/cephfs/README.md#how-to-use-it

user.description: user is optional: User is the rados user name, default is admin More info: https://examples.k8s.io/volumes/cephfs/README.md#how-to-use-it

k8s.io.api.core.v1.CinderVolumeSource.description:
Volume backed by an OpenStack Cinder block-storage volume. Legacy
in-tree driver; prefer the cinder.csi.openstack.org CSI driver.

fsType.description: fsType is the filesystem type to mount. Must be a filesystem type supported by the host operating system. Examples: "ext4", "xfs", "ntfs". Implicitly inferred to be "ext4" if unspecified. More info: https://examples.k8s.io/mysql-cinder-pd/README.md

readOnly.description: readOnly defaults to false (read/write). ReadOnly here will force the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts. More info: https://examples.k8s.io/mysql-cinder-pd/README.md

secretRef.description: secretRef is optional: points to a secret object containing parameters used to connect to OpenStack.

volumeID.description: volumeID used to identify the volume in cinder. More info: https://examples.k8s.io/mysql-cinder-pd/README.md

k8s.io.api.core.v1.ClusterTrustBundlePro.description:
Projected volume entry that exposes a ClusterTrustBundle's CA
certificates into the Pod's filesystem, selected by name or signer.

labelSelector.description: Select all ClusterTrustBundles that match this label selector.  Only has effect if signerName is set.  Mutually-exclusive with name.  If unset, interpreted as "match nothing".  If set but empty, interpreted as "match everything".

name.description: Select a single ClusterTrustBundle by object name.  Mutually-exclusive with signerName and labelSelector.

optional.description: If true, don't block pod startup if the referenced ClusterTrustBundle(s) aren't available.  If using name, then the named ClusterTrustBundle is allowed not to exist.  If using signerName, then the combination of signerName and labelSelector is allowed to match zero ClusterTrustBundles.

path.description: Relative path from the volume root to write the bundle.

signerName.description: Select all ClusterTrustBundles that match this signer name. Mutually-exclusive with name.  The contents of all selected ClusterTrustBundles will be unified and deduplicated.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.ConfigMapKeySelector.description:
Pulls one key from a ConfigMap into an env var or other consumer.
`name` references the ConfigMap by name in the Pod's namespace;
`key` is the data key; `optional: true` lets the Pod start even if
the ConfigMap or key is missing (the env var is unset). Used inside
env-var `valueFrom.configMapKeyRef`. The chart provides this shape
via the typed `env[].valueFrom` field.

name.description: Name of the referent. This field is effectively required, but due to backwards compatibility is allowed to be empty. Instances of this type with an empty value here are almost certainly wrong. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names/#names

optional.description: Specify whether the ConfigMap or its key must be defined

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `optional: false` (the default) for a ConfigMap that might not exist — the Pod fails to start with `CreateContainerConfigError`.
Pointing at a ConfigMap in a different namespace — references are namespace-local; the env var resolves to empty and the Pod usually fails on the application side.
Treating the key as case-insensitive — ConfigMap data keys are case-sensitive; `LOG_LEVEL` and `log_level` are distinct.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.ConfigMapKeySelector.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use to inject a single ConfigMap value as an env var with a
different name than the key. For bulk injection of every key as
env vars with matching names, use `envFrom.configMapRef` (envFrom
doesn't let you rename).

k8s.io.api.core.v1.ConfigMapProjection.description:
One entry inside a ProjectedVolumeSource that pulls data from a
ConfigMap. Shape mirrors ConfigMapVolumeSource: `name` references
the ConfigMap, `items` optionally restricts to specific keys with
per-item paths and modes, `optional: true` makes the projection
non-blocking if the ConfigMap is absent. Lives inside a
VolumeProjection's `configMap` slot.

items.description: items if unspecified, each key-value pair in the Data field of the referenced ConfigMap will be projected into the volume as a file whose name is the key and content is the value. If specified, the listed keys will be projected into the specified paths, and unlisted keys will not be present. If a key is specified which is not present in the ConfigMap, the volume setup will error unless it is marked optional. Paths must be relative and may not contain the '..' path or start with '..'.

name.description: Name of the referent. This field is effectively required, but due to backwards compatibility is allowed to be empty. Instances of this type with an empty value here are almost certainly wrong. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names/#names

optional.description: optional specify whether the ConfigMap or its keys must be defined

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `optional: false` (the default) for a ConfigMap that may not exist yet — the whole projected volume fails to mount and the Pod stays Pending.
Overlapping `path` values across multiple projections in the same projected volume — later projections silently overwrite earlier ones.
Using this outside a projected volume — ConfigMapProjection is only valid as a VolumeProjection slot; standalone use is rejected.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.ConfigMapProjection.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use only inside a `projected` volume's `sources[]` when you're
combining ConfigMap data with other sources (secrets, downward
API, service-account tokens). For a standalone ConfigMap mount
use ConfigMapVolumeSource directly.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.ConfigMapVolumeSource.description:
Volume that materializes a ConfigMap's data as files inside the Pod.
`name` references the ConfigMap by name in the Pod's namespace.
Without `items` every key in the ConfigMap becomes a file at the
mount path; with `items` only the listed keys are projected, each
with an optional `path` (relative to the mount path) and `mode`
(file-permission bits). `defaultMode` (e.g. `0644`) is applied to
all files not given a per-item mode. `optional: true` lets the Pod
start even if the ConfigMap is missing (the directory is empty);
`optional: false` (the default) keeps the Pod Pending until it
exists. ConfigMap updates propagate to mounted files atomically
within ~60s — but only when the whole volume is mounted, not when
individual keys are mounted via `subPath` in the container.

defaultMode.description: defaultMode is optional: mode bits used to set permissions on created files by default. Must be an octal value between 0000 and 0777 or a decimal value between 0 and 511. YAML accepts both octal and decimal values, JSON requires decimal values for mode bits. Defaults to 0644. Directories within the path are not affected by this setting. This might be in conflict with other options that affect the file mode, like fsGroup, and the result can be other mode bits set.

items.description: items if unspecified, each key-value pair in the Data field of the referenced ConfigMap will be projected into the volume as a file whose name is the key and content is the value. If specified, the listed keys will be projected into the specified paths, and unlisted keys will not be present. If a key is specified which is not present in the ConfigMap, the volume setup will error unless it is marked optional. Paths must be relative and may not contain the '..' path or start with '..'.

name.description: Name of the referent. This field is effectively required, but due to backwards compatibility is allowed to be empty. Instances of this type with an empty value here are almost certainly wrong. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names/#names

optional.description: optional specify whether the ConfigMap or its keys must be defined

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using `subPath: <key>` in the matching `volumeMounts[]` to land the file as a single entry — Kubernetes uses non-atomic rename for subPath mounts, so ConfigMap updates *never* propagate; the file is frozen at Pod start.
Setting `optional: false` (the default) for a ConfigMap that doesn't exist yet — Pod stays Pending; either create the ConfigMap first or set `optional: true`.
Setting `defaultMode` as decimal (e.g. `420` for 0644) and getting unexpected permissions — modes are octal; specify with leading zero or use the integer equivalent carefully.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.ConfigMapVolumeSource.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for non-secret configuration files. For credentials use
`SecretVolumeSource`. For combining ConfigMap + Secret + downward
API into one mount, use a `ProjectedVolumeSource`. Avoid the
`subPath` mount style on container if you need updates to
propagate.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.DownwardAPIProjection.description:
One entry inside a ProjectedVolumeSource that exposes Pod metadata or
container resource info as files. `items[]` is a list of
DownwardAPIVolumeFile entries — each writing one target `path` from
either `fieldRef` (Pod-level) or `resourceFieldRef` (container
resources). Identical content shape to DownwardAPIVolumeSource but
lives inside a VolumeProjection rather than as a standalone volume.

items.description: Items is a list of DownwardAPIVolume file

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using a `fieldPath` not allowed in volumes (e.g. `status.podIP`) — works in env vars but rejected in volume sources by the API.
Omitting `containerName` on `resourceFieldRef` — required for resource selectors; without it the projection fails validation.
Overlapping paths across projections — last one wins silently.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.DownwardAPIProjection.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use inside a projected volume to surface pod identity (name,
namespace, labels, annotations, UID) or container resource
requests/limits alongside other projected sources. For standalone
use prefer DownwardAPIVolumeSource.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.DownwardAPIVolumeFile.description:
One file entry inside a DownwardAPIVolumeSource or DownwardAPIProjection.
`path` is the target filename (relative, no `..`, no leading `/`).
Populate exactly one of `fieldRef` (for Pod-level fields like
`metadata.name`, `metadata.namespace`, `metadata.labels`,
`metadata.annotations`, `spec.nodeName`, `spec.serviceAccountName`)
or `resourceFieldRef` (for container resource requests/limits with a
`containerName` + `resource` + optional `divisor`). Optional `mode`
overrides the volume's `defaultMode` for this file.

fieldRef.description: Required: Selects a field of the pod: only annotations, labels, name, namespace and uid are supported.

mode.description: Optional: mode bits used to set permissions on this file, must be an octal value between 0000 and 0777 or a decimal value between 0 and 511. YAML accepts both octal and decimal values, JSON requires decimal values for mode bits. If not specified, the volume defaultMode will be used. This might be in conflict with other options that affect the file mode, like fsGroup, and the result can be other mode bits set.

path.description: Required: Path is  the relative path name of the file to be created. Must not be absolute or contain the '..' path. Must be utf-8 encoded. The first item of the relative path must not start with '..'

resourceFieldRef.description: Selects a resource of the container: only resources limits and requests (limits.cpu, limits.memory, requests.cpu and requests.memory) are currently supported.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using `status.podIP` or `status.hostIP` in `fieldRef.fieldPath` — these are env-var-only; the volume rejects them at admission.
Omitting `containerName` on `resourceFieldRef` — required; the field doesn't have a sensible default since each container has its own resources.
Specifying both `fieldRef` and `resourceFieldRef` in the same entry — exactly one must be set; the API rejects entries with both or neither.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.DownwardAPIVolumeFile.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use as the entries of a downward API volume's `items[]`. Allowed
`fieldRef` values are a *restricted* subset compared to env-var
downward API (e.g. `status.podIP` works only as an env var, not in
volumes); pick env vars for runtime status, volumes for identity
and resources.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.DownwardAPIVolumeSour.description:
Volume that exposes Pod metadata and container resource info as files
inside the Pod, without requiring the workload to call the API server.
`items[]` is a list of DownwardAPIVolumeFile entries — each one
writes a target `path` (relative to the mount path) with content
taken either from `fieldRef` (Pod-level fields like `metadata.name`,
`metadata.labels`, `spec.nodeName`) or `resourceFieldRef` (container
resource requests/limits). `defaultMode` applies to files without
per-item modes. Useful for shipping pod identity to a workload that
needs to log it but shouldn't have API server access.

defaultMode.description: Optional: mode bits to use on created files by default. Must be a Optional: mode bits used to set permissions on created files by default. Must be an octal value between 0000 and 0777 or a decimal value between 0 and 511. YAML accepts both octal and decimal values, JSON requires decimal values for mode bits. Defaults to 0644. Directories within the path are not affected by this setting. This might be in conflict with other options that affect the file mode, like fsGroup, and the result can be other mode bits set.

items.description: Items is a list of downward API volume file

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using a `fieldPath` Kubernetes doesn't allow (e.g. `status.podIP` works only in env vars, not in volumes — the API rejects it).
Forgetting `containerName` on `resourceFieldRef` — the field is required for resource selectors; without it the volume fails validation.
Mounting via `subPath` and expecting label updates to propagate — like ConfigMap/Secret mounts, subPath bypasses atomic update, so label changes never appear.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.DownwardAPIVolumeSour.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use to expose pod identity (name, namespace, labels), node name, or
container resource requests/limits as files. For env-variable
access to the same data, use `valueFrom.fieldRef` /
`valueFrom.resourceFieldRef` on container env — simpler when you
only need one or two values.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.EmptyDirVolumeSource.description:
Empty scratch volume created when the Pod is assigned to a node and
destroyed when the Pod is removed from that node — so it persists
across container restarts inside the same Pod, but *not* across
rescheduling. `medium: ""` (the default) backs it with the node's
default filesystem (typically a directory under
`/var/lib/kubelet/pods/`); `medium: Memory` backs it with tmpfs,
which is fast but counts against the Pod's memory limit and reduces
schedulable memory. `sizeLimit` caps total usage and kicks in at
eviction time — workloads exceeding the cap are evicted, not blocked
at write time.

medium.description: medium represents what type of storage medium should back this directory. The default is "" which means to use the node's default medium. Must be an empty string (default) or Memory. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#emptydir

sizeLimit.description: sizeLimit is the total amount of local storage required for this EmptyDir volume. The size limit is also applicable for memory medium. The maximum usage on memory medium EmptyDir would be the minimum value between the SizeLimit specified here and the sum of memory limits of all containers in a pod. The default is nil which means that the limit is undefined. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#emptydir

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using emptyDir for data that must survive Pod rescheduling — rescheduling to another node returns an empty directory. Use a PVC.
Setting `medium: Memory` without raising the container `memory` limit — tmpfs writes count against the limit; the Pod OOM-kills as the volume fills.
Forgetting `sizeLimit` on a shared write target — runaway writes can fill the node's ephemeral storage and impact other Pods on the same node.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.EmptyDirVolumeSource.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for scratch space that lives with the Pod — caches, temporary
output, shared file between init and main containers. For data
that must survive Pod rescheduling use a PVC. For tmpfs-backed
scratch (faster, sized in RAM) set `medium: Memory`; remember the
memory counts against the container limit.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.EphemeralVolumeSource.description:
Inline PVC creation — the kubelet materializes a per-Pod
PersistentVolumeClaim from `volumeClaimTemplate` at Pod creation,
and deletes it when the Pod is deleted. The generated PVC's name is
`<pod-name>-<volume-name>` and it inherits the Pod's namespace.
Behaves like an EmptyDir bound to durable storage: data does *not*
survive Pod rescheduling (a new Pod gets a fresh PVC) but does
survive container restarts inside the same Pod. Requires a
dynamic provisioner (CSI driver with a StorageClass) for the
claim to bind; static PV provisioning is incompatible with
ephemeral volumes.

volumeClaimTemplate.description:
Will be used to create a stand-alone PVC to provision the volume. The pod in which this EphemeralVolumeSource is embedded will be the owner of the PVC, i.e. the PVC will be deleted together with the pod.  The name of the PVC will be `<pod name>-<volume name>` where `<volume name>` is the name from the `PodSpec.Volumes` array entry. Pod validation will reject the pod if the concatenated name is not valid for a PVC (for example, too long).

An existing PVC with that name that is not owned by the pod will *not* be used for the pod to avoid using an unrelated volume by mistake. Starting the pod is then blocked until the unrelated PVC is removed. If such a pre-created PVC is meant to be used by the pod, the PVC has to updated with an owner reference to the pod once the pod exists. Normally this should not be necessary, but it may be useful when manually reconstructing a broken cluster.

This field is read-only and no changes will be made by Kubernetes to the PVC after it has been created.

Required, must not be nil.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Forgetting that the PVC is *deleted with the Pod* — assuming data persists across Deployment rollouts is wrong; rollouts replace Pods, which destroys ephemeral PVCs.
Using `accessModes: [ReadWriteMany]` on a provider that doesn't support it — the PVC stays Pending and the Pod never starts.

Setting `storageClassName: ""` (empty string) intending to use the cluster default — empty string means "no storage class" (use static binding), which usually breaks dynamic provisioning. Omit the field entirely to use the default class.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.EphemeralVolumeSource.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when each Pod replica needs its own durable scratch volume that
should disappear with the Pod — large workspace dirs, per-Pod
caches that benefit from real storage rather than tmpfs/emptyDir.
For shared durable storage across replicas, use a regular
PersistentVolumeClaim with `ReadWriteMany`. For shared scratch, use
`EmptyDirVolumeSource`.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.ExecAction.description:
Probe / lifecycle-hook action that runs a command inside the container
using the kubelet's `exec` (essentially `kubectl exec` semantics).
`command` is the argv list — no shell, so `["sh", "-c", "..."]` is
needed for shell features. The command runs with the container's
working directory and user; exit code 0 is success, anything else
is failure. Exec probes are the most expensive probe type (fork +
process spawn per probe interval); for HTTP services prefer
`httpGet`, for raw TCP listeners prefer `tcpSocket`.

command.description: Command is the command line to execute inside the container, the working directory for the command  is root ('/') in the container's filesystem. The command is simply exec'd, it is not run inside a shell, so traditional shell instructions ('|', etc) won't work. To use a shell, you need to explicitly call out to that shell. Exit status of 0 is treated as live/healthy and non-zero is unhealthy.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Writing `command: ["pg_isready -U postgres"]` (one string) instead of `["pg_isready", "-U", "postgres"]` — the kubelet does no shell parsing; the single string is treated as the executable name and `exec` fails.
Running expensive checks (full DB queries, full cache walks) on every probe — exec probes execute per interval; this becomes a measurable load source.
Probing a process started via initContainer side effects — initContainers run before the main container, but exec probes only target the main container.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.ExecAction.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when the workload can't expose an HTTP or TCP health endpoint —
e.g. CLI tools, databases probed via their native client
(`pg_isready`, `redis-cli ping`), or workloads that signal health by
writing a sentinel file. For HTTP services use `httpGet` instead;
for TCP listeners use `tcpSocket`. Both are dramatically cheaper.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.FCVolumeSource.description:
Volume backed by a Fibre Channel LUN. Specifies target WWNs (or WWIDs)
and the LUN number to attach.

fsType.description: fsType is the filesystem type to mount. Must be a filesystem type supported by the host operating system. Ex. "ext4", "xfs", "ntfs". Implicitly inferred to be "ext4" if unspecified.

lun.description: lun is Optional: FC target lun number

readOnly.description: readOnly is Optional: Defaults to false (read/write). ReadOnly here will force the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts.

targetWWNs.description: targetWWNs is Optional: FC target worldwide names (WWNs)

wwids.description: wwids Optional: FC volume world wide identifiers (wwids) Either wwids or combination of targetWWNs and lun must be set, but not both simultaneously.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.FlexVolumeSource.description:
Volume served by a FlexVolume out-of-tree driver. Deprecated in favor of
CSI; retained for backwards compatibility only.

driver.description: driver is the name of the driver to use for this volume.

fsType.description: fsType is the filesystem type to mount. Must be a filesystem type supported by the host operating system. Ex. "ext4", "xfs", "ntfs". The default filesystem depends on FlexVolume script.

options.description: options is Optional: this field holds extra command options if any.

readOnly.description: readOnly is Optional: defaults to false (read/write). ReadOnly here will force the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts.

secretRef.description: secretRef is Optional: secretRef is reference to the secret object containing sensitive information to pass to the plugin scripts. This may be empty if no secret object is specified. If the secret object contains more than one secret, all secrets are passed to the plugin scripts.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.FlockerVolumeSource.description:
Volume backed by Flocker (ClusterHQ). Largely defunct upstream;
retained as an in-tree volume source for historical clusters.

datasetName.description: datasetName is Name of the dataset stored as metadata -> name on the dataset for Flocker should be considered as deprecated

datasetUUID.description: datasetUUID is the UUID of the dataset. This is unique identifier of a Flocker dataset

k8s.io.api.core.v1.GCEPersistentDiskVolu.description:
Volume backed by an existing GCE Persistent Disk. Legacy in-tree driver;
prefer the pd.csi.storage.gke.io CSI driver for new workloads.

fsType.description: fsType is filesystem type of the volume that you want to mount. Tip: Ensure that the filesystem type is supported by the host operating system. Examples: "ext4", "xfs", "ntfs". Implicitly inferred to be "ext4" if unspecified. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#gcepersistentdisk

partition.description: partition is the partition in the volume that you want to mount. If omitted, the default is to mount by volume name. Examples: For volume /dev/sda1, you specify the partition as "1". Similarly, the volume partition for /dev/sda is "0" (or you can leave the property empty). More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#gcepersistentdisk

pdName.description: pdName is unique name of the PD resource in GCE. Used to identify the disk in GCE. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#gcepersistentdisk

readOnly.description: readOnly here will force the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts. Defaults to false. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#gcepersistentdisk

k8s.io.api.core.v1.GitRepoVolumeSource.description:
Deprecated volume type that clones a Git repository into an EmptyDir at
Pod startup. Use an initContainer with git clone instead.

directory.description: directory is the target directory name. Must not contain or start with '..'.  If '.' is supplied, the volume directory will be the git repository.  Otherwise, if specified, the volume will contain the git repository in the subdirectory with the given name.

revision.description: revision is the commit hash for the specified revision.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.GlusterfsVolumeSource.description:
Volume backed by a Glusterfs mount. References a known endpoints object,
a volume path, and an optional read-only flag.

endpoints.description: endpoints is the endpoint name that details Glusterfs topology.

path.description: path is the Glusterfs volume path. More info: https://examples.k8s.io/volumes/glusterfs/README.md#create-a-pod

readOnly.description: readOnly here will force the Glusterfs volume to be mounted with read-only permissions. Defaults to false. More info: https://examples.k8s.io/volumes/glusterfs/README.md#create-a-pod

k8s.io.api.core.v1.HTTPGetAction.description:
Probe / lifecycle-hook action that issues an HTTP(S) GET to the
container. `port` is a port number or named port from
`containers[].ports[]`; `path` is the request path (default `/`);
`host` is rarely set (defaults to the Pod IP) and is only useful
when probing through a specific Host header; `scheme` is `HTTP`
(default) or `HTTPS` (no TLS validation — the kubelet does not
verify server certs); `httpHeaders[]` adds headers, typically for
Authorization or to override Host. Status codes 200–399 are
success; 4xx/5xx are failure; the kubelet does not follow redirects.

host.description: Host name to connect to, defaults to the pod IP. You probably want to set "Host" in httpHeaders instead.

httpHeaders.description: Custom headers to set in the request. HTTP allows repeated headers.

path.description: Path to access on the HTTP server.

port.description: Name or number of the port to access on the container. Number must be in the range 1 to 65535. Name must be an IANA_SVC_NAME.

scheme.description: Scheme to use for connecting to the host. Defaults to HTTP.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Pointing the probe at the main business path (e.g. `/`) and seeing probe traffic mask real metrics or load latency-sensitive code — always use a dedicated probe path that is cheap and side-effect-free.
Using `scheme: HTTPS` against a self-signed cert and assuming the kubelet validates it — it doesn't, but external monitoring will, so don't conflate the two.
Mixing up readiness and liveness probes — readiness controls Service endpoints; liveness restarts containers. Pointing both at the same heavy path can cascade restarts during transient load.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.HTTPGetAction.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for any HTTP service — preferred over `tcpSocket` because a
passing HTTP GET proves the app actually responds, not just that
the TCP listener is up. Reserve a dedicated lightweight health
endpoint (don't probe `/`) to avoid load. Use `tcpSocket` only for
non-HTTP services or where adding HTTP is impractical.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.HTTPHeader.description:
One `{name, value}` header pair attached to an HTTPGetAction's
`httpHeaders[]`. Used to override the implicit Host header (kubelet
sets it to the Pod IP by default), inject an Authorization token
for protected health endpoints, or pass any other header the
health endpoint requires. Header names follow HTTP rules — case-
insensitive at the protocol level, but most apps treat them as
lowercased internally.

name.description: The header field name. This will be canonicalized upon output, so case-variant names will be understood as the same header.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Hardcoding a token in the manifest — it lives in the API server in plain text; for real auth tokens, prefer an unauthenticated internal health endpoint.
Using `Host` header to probe a different service — the request still goes to the configured `port` on the configured `host` (or Pod IP); the header doesn't redirect.
Spelling header names inconsistently with what the server expects when the server is case-sensitive about names (against the HTTP spec, but real apps do this).
k8s.io.api.core.v1.HTTPHeader.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use to send auth tokens to protected probes, or to send a specific
Host header when virtual hosting is involved. For probes that
should bypass auth entirely, expose a dedicated unauthenticated
`/healthz` endpoint inside the pod instead of injecting tokens.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.HostPathVolumeSource.description:
Mounts a file or directory from the underlying node's filesystem
directly into the Pod. `path` is the node-local path; `type`
validates how that path must exist (`Directory`, `File`,
`DirectoryOrCreate`, `FileOrCreate`, `Socket`, `CharDevice`,
`BlockDevice`, or `""` for no validation). HostPath couples the Pod
to a specific node (it has access to that node's filesystem) and is
a frequent source of security and portability problems — most
Pod Security Standards `baseline` and above forbid it outright. Use
only for genuine node-level work (DaemonSet log collectors,
monitoring agents reading `/proc`, CSI driver pods).

path.description: path of the directory on the host. If the path is a symlink, it will follow the link to the real path. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#hostpath

type.description: type for HostPath Volume Defaults to "" More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#hostpath

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using HostPath for application data and being surprised by data loss when the Pod reschedules — Pods are not pinned to nodes by default, so the new node has empty (or different) host data.

Setting `type: ""` (no validation) and getting a silent EmptyDir-like behavior when the path doesn't exist (with `DirectoryOrCreate`/`FileOrCreate` the kubelet creates it; with `Directory` it errors out).
Mounting `/` or `/etc` and tripping Pod Security policies — `restricted` and `baseline` profiles forbid HostPath; the Pod is rejected at admission.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.HostPathVolumeSource.x-agent-when-to-use:
Reserve for node-level infrastructure (log shippers, node exporters,
CSI plugins). For application data prefer a PVC; for scratch use an
EmptyDir; for shared cluster state use a ConfigMap/Secret. If you
think a normal application needs HostPath, you almost certainly
want a different volume type.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.ISCSIVolumeSource.description:
Volume backed by an iSCSI LUN. Configures target portal, IQN, LUN, and
optional CHAP authentication via a referenced Secret.

chapAuthDiscovery.description: chapAuthDiscovery defines whether support iSCSI Discovery CHAP authentication

chapAuthSession.description: chapAuthSession defines whether support iSCSI Session CHAP authentication

fsType.description: fsType is the filesystem type of the volume that you want to mount. Tip: Ensure that the filesystem type is supported by the host operating system. Examples: "ext4", "xfs", "ntfs". Implicitly inferred to be "ext4" if unspecified. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#iscsi

initiatorName.description: initiatorName is the custom iSCSI Initiator Name. If initiatorName is specified with iscsiInterface simultaneously, new iSCSI interface <target portal>:<volume name> will be created for the connection.

iqn.description: iqn is the target iSCSI Qualified Name.

iscsiInterface.description: iscsiInterface is the interface Name that uses an iSCSI transport. Defaults to 'default' (tcp).

lun.description: lun represents iSCSI Target Lun number.

portals.description: portals is the iSCSI Target Portal List. The portal is either an IP or ip_addr:port if the port is other than default (typically TCP ports 860 and 3260).

readOnly.description: readOnly here will force the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts. Defaults to false.

secretRef.description: secretRef is the CHAP Secret for iSCSI target and initiator authentication

targetPortal.description: targetPortal is iSCSI Target Portal. The Portal is either an IP or ip_addr:port if the port is other than default (typically TCP ports 860 and 3260).

k8s.io.api.core.v1.ImageVolumeSource.description:
Volume populated from an OCI image artifact. The image content is
exposed as a read-only filesystem inside the Pod.

pullPolicy.description: Policy for pulling OCI objects. Possible values are: Always: the kubelet always attempts to pull the reference. Container creation will fail If the pull fails. Never: the kubelet never pulls the reference and only uses a local image or artifact. Container creation will fail if the reference isn't present. IfNotPresent: the kubelet pulls if the reference isn't already present on disk. Container creation will fail if the reference isn't present and the pull fails. Defaults to Always if :latest tag is specified, or IfNotPresent otherwise.

reference.description: Required: Image or artifact reference to be used. Behaves in the same way as pod.spec.containers[*].image. Pull secrets will be assembled in the same way as for the container image by looking up node credentials, SA image pull secrets, and pod spec image pull secrets. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/containers/images This field is optional to allow higher level config management to default or override container images in workload controllers like Deployments and StatefulSets.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.KeyToPath.description:
One per-key mapping inside a ConfigMapVolumeSource, SecretVolumeSource,
ConfigMapProjection, or SecretProjection's `items[]` list. `key`
picks the source key from the ConfigMap/Secret, `path` is the
target filename relative to the volume mount path (must not be
absolute or contain `..`), and optional `mode` overrides the volume's
`defaultMode` for this single file. Listing items at all *restricts*
the volume to only those keys; omit `items` to project every key.

key.description: key is the key to project.

mode.description: mode is Optional: mode bits used to set permissions on this file. Must be an octal value between 0000 and 0777 or a decimal value between 0 and 511. YAML accepts both octal and decimal values, JSON requires decimal values for mode bits. If not specified, the volume defaultMode will be used. This might be in conflict with other options that affect the file mode, like fsGroup, and the result can be other mode bits set.

path.description: path is the relative path of the file to map the key to. May not be an absolute path. May not contain the path element '..'. May not start with the string '..'.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using an absolute path or `..` in `path` — the kubelet rejects the mount at admission; paths must be relative and stay inside the volume.
Listing `items` and then forgetting one of the source keys — that key is silently absent from the volume; listing items means opt-in, not append.
Specifying `mode` as decimal (e.g. `420` for 0644) — modes are octal in YAML; the parser converts decimal correctly only when written as `0644`.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.KeyToPath.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when you only need a subset of keys from a ConfigMap/Secret, or
when you want to rename keys at mount time, or when a single file
needs stricter permissions than the volume's `defaultMode`. To
project everything as-is, omit `items` entirely.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.Lifecycle.description:
Per-container hooks the kubelet invokes around the container's lifetime.
`postStart` fires immediately after the container is created (no
guarantee it runs before the entrypoint, and a slow hook delays the
container reaching the Running state). `preStop` is by far the more
common use: it runs synchronously when a Pod is asked to terminate and
the container stays in Terminating until the hook returns or the Pod's
`terminationGracePeriodSeconds` elapses, whichever comes first — once
the grace period is exhausted the kubelet sends SIGKILL regardless.
Use preStop to drain in-flight requests, deregister from a service
registry, or sleep long enough for endpoints removal to propagate.

postStart.description: PostStart is called immediately after a container is created. If the handler fails, the container is terminated and restarted according to its restart policy. Other management of the container blocks until the hook completes. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/containers/container-lifecycle-hooks/#container-hooks

preStop.description: PreStop is called immediately before a container is terminated due to an API request or management event such as liveness/startup probe failure, preemption, resource contention, etc. The handler is not called if the container crashes or exits. The Pod's termination grace period countdown begins before the PreStop hook is executed. Regardless of the outcome of the handler, the container will eventually terminate within the Pod's termination grace period (unless delayed by finalizers). Other management of the container blocks until the hook completes or until the termination grace period is reached. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/containers/container-lifecycle-hooks/#container-hooks

stopSignal.description: StopSignal defines which signal will be sent to a container when it is being stopped. If not specified, the default is defined by the container runtime in use. StopSignal can only be set for Pods with a non-empty .spec.os.name

x-agent-common-mistakes: preStop sleep or work that exceeds `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` — the kubelet sends SIGKILL on expiry, the hook's effect is lost, and in-flight requests fail. Always keep preStop duration < grace period.

Using `httpGet` for preStop against an endpoint the readiness probe has already taken out of rotation — the hook can still hit it locally, but pairing httpGet with a slow upstream chain can stall the entire grace period.
Relying on `postStart` ordering vs the entrypoint — Kubernetes does not guarantee postStart runs before the container's main process, so the hook cannot safely assume the app is initialized.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.Lifecycle.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use `preStop` whenever the workload accepts external traffic — the
`sleep N` pattern bridges the gap between SIGTERM and endpoint
removal. Use `postStart` only for genuinely fast initialization that
cannot live in an init container; init containers are the correct
tool for warm-up work that must complete before main traffic.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.LifecycleHandler.description:
Action shape for `postStart` and `preStop` lifecycle hooks. Populate
exactly one of `exec`, `httpGet`, or `sleep` (the latter added in
Kubernetes 1.29 as a simpler way to express the "wait N seconds"
pattern that used to require `exec: ["sleep", "N"]`). `tcpSocket`
is *not* available here — lifecycle hooks support a strict subset
of probe actions. Hook failures are logged but don't restart the
container directly; `preStop` blocking past
`terminationGracePeriodSeconds` causes SIGKILL.

exec.description: Exec specifies a command to execute in the container.

httpGet.description: HTTPGet specifies an HTTP GET request to perform.

sleep.description: Sleep represents a duration that the container should sleep.

tcpSocket.description: Deprecated. TCPSocket is NOT supported as a LifecycleHandler and kept for backward compatibility. There is no validation of this field and lifecycle hooks will fail at runtime when it is specified.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Trying to use `tcpSocket` here — not supported in LifecycleHandler; only `exec`, `httpGet`, and `sleep` (1.29+) are valid.
Letting `preStop` work exceed `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` — the kubelet SIGKILLs at expiry regardless of hook progress; tune grace period accordingly.
Setting `postStart` to `httpGet` against the workload's own port and racing the entrypoint — there is no ordering guarantee between postStart and entrypoint; the GET may hit a not-yet-listening port.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.LifecycleHandler.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use `sleep` for the canonical "wait for endpoint removal to
propagate" preStop hook (Kubernetes 1.29+). On older clusters use
`exec: ["sleep", "15"]` instead. Use `httpGet` to ping a graceful-
shutdown endpoint inside the workload; use `exec` for non-HTTP
apps that drain via CLI.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.LocalObjectReference.description:
Name-only reference to another object in the same namespace. Just a
single `name` field — there is no kind/apiVersion because the
consumer site already implies them. The most common use is
`imagePullSecrets` on a PodSpec (refers to a Secret), but the shape
reappears in many places (env-var `valueFrom.configMapRef`,
`envFrom.secretRef`, several volume types, etc.).

name.description: Name of the referent. This field is effectively required, but due to backwards compatibility is allowed to be empty. Instances of this type with an empty value here are almost certainly wrong. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names/#names

x-agent-common-mistakes: Pointing at an object in another namespace — LocalObjectReference is namespace-local; the API silently won't find the cross-namespace object.
Adding `kind` or `apiVersion` thinking the reference needs them — these fields don't exist on LocalObjectReference; the consumer site already knows the type.
Forgetting `name` (writing `{}`) — most schema validators reject this, but raw Kubernetes treats an empty reference as 'no reference' silently.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.LocalObjectReference.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use wherever the API asks for a LocalObjectReference. The chart
surfaces `imagePullSecrets:` at the deployment level, which expects
a list of these. For Secrets referenced by env-from, use the
envFrom shape; for ConfigMaps similarly.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.NFSVolumeSource.description:
Volume backed by an NFS export. Specifies server address, exported path,
and an optional read-only flag.

path.description: path that is exported by the NFS server. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#nfs

readOnly.description: readOnly here will force the NFS export to be mounted with read-only permissions. Defaults to false. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#nfs

server.description: server is the hostname or IP address of the NFS server. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes#nfs

k8s.io.api.core.v1.NodeAffinity.description:
Scheduling rules expressed over node labels. Two independent fields:
`requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` (a hard NodeSelector
— pods stay Pending unless at least one term matches) and
`preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` (a list of weighted
preferences — the scheduler biases placement but does not block).
Both `IgnoredDuringExecution` suffixes mean Kubernetes does NOT
reschedule running pods when node labels change after placement;
there is no `RequiredDuringExecution` variant in mainline Kubernetes.
For simple key=value matching prefer `nodeSelector` for brevity.

preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringEx.description: The scheduler will prefer to schedule pods to nodes that satisfy the affinity expressions specified by this field, but it may choose a node that violates one or more of the expressions. The node that is most preferred is the one with the greatest sum of weights, i.e. for each node that meets all of the scheduling requirements (resource request, requiredDuringScheduling affinity expressions, etc.), compute a sum by iterating through the elements of this field and adding "weight" to the sum if the node matches the corresponding matchExpressions; the node(s) with the highest sum are the most preferred.

requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExe.description: If the affinity requirements specified by this field are not met at scheduling time, the pod will not be scheduled onto the node. If the affinity requirements specified by this field cease to be met at some point during pod execution (e.g. due to an update), the system may or may not try to eventually evict the pod from its node.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using `required...` for what should be a preference — pods stay Pending forever if no node matches; switch to `preferred...` with a `weight`.
Combining nodeSelector and a NodeAffinity term for the same constraint — they AND together at scheduling time; pods must satisfy both.
Forgetting that `IgnoredDuringExecution` means running pods are not evicted when labels change — relabeling a node won't move existing workloads.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.NodeAffinity.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use `required...` when scheduling onto the wrong nodes is unsafe (GPU
workloads, license-constrained nodes, dedicated tiers). Use
`preferred...` for soft preferences with fallback. For the simple
`key=value` case `nodeSelector` is shorter and equivalent.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.NodeSelector.description:
Disjunctive (OR) collection of NodeSelectorTerms. Wraps the hard
`requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` rule in NodeAffinity:
a node satisfies the selector when *at least one* term matches. Each
term is itself a conjunction (AND) of `matchExpressions` and
optional `matchFields`. This is a different shape from the flat
`nodeSelector` map on PodSpec — that one is map-style key=value AND;
this one is list-style OR of ANDs and is much more expressive.

nodeSelectorTerms.description: Required. A list of node selector terms. The terms are ORed.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Confusing semantics: a single term with multiple `matchExpressions` is AND, but multiple terms in `nodeSelectorTerms` are OR. Beginners often flip this and produce a rule that's much narrower or broader than intended.
Omitting `nodeSelectorTerms` entirely — an empty NodeSelector matches *no* nodes; the pod is Pending forever.
Using `In: []` with an empty values list — rejected by the API, but with `NotIn: []` Kubernetes treats it as 'never matches' which is often surprising.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.NodeSelector.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use inside `nodeAffinity.requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution`
whenever the hard rule can't be expressed as a single AND of
key=value pairs — e.g. "us-east-1a with m5.large OR us-east-1b
with m5.large". For pure AND of key=value, `nodeSelector` on the
PodSpec is shorter.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.NodeSelectorRequireme.description:
One match expression against a node label. `key` is the label name,
`operator` is one of `In`, `NotIn`, `Exists`, `DoesNotExist`, `Gt`,
`Lt`, and `values` is a list whose meaning depends on the operator:
with `In`/`NotIn` it must be non-empty; with `Exists`/`DoesNotExist`
it must be empty; with `Gt`/`Lt` it must be exactly one integer
string. `Gt`/`Lt` compare numeric labels only — labels are strings
in Kubernetes and the comparator parses them as integers, so
decimal/non-numeric values fail validation.

key.description: The label key that the selector applies to.

operator.description: Represents a key's relationship to a set of values. Valid operators are In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist. Gt, and Lt.

values.description: An array of string values. If the operator is In or NotIn, the values array must be non-empty. If the operator is Exists or DoesNotExist, the values array must be empty. If the operator is Gt or Lt, the values array must have a single element, which will be interpreted as an integer. This array is replaced during a strategic merge patch.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using `In` without a non-empty `values` list (or `Exists` with a `values` list) — the API validates this and rejects the pod.
Using `Gt`/`Lt` against a label that isn't strictly integer-valued — comparison is numeric, non-numeric labels never satisfy the expression.

Forgetting `NotIn` matches nodes that *also* lack the label — set theory: 'not in {x,y}' includes 'has no value at all'. If you want 'has the label but not these values', combine `Exists` AND `NotIn`.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.NodeSelectorRequireme.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use as a leaf inside a NodeSelectorTerm's `matchExpressions` (or
`matchFields`) — list as many requirements as needed; all of them
AND together within the term. For simple equality across the whole
PodSpec, prefer `nodeSelector` for brevity.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.NodeSelectorTerm.description:
One AND-conjunction of node requirements. Pairs `matchExpressions`
(against node labels — the common case) with optional `matchFields`
(against node field paths like `metadata.name`). All entries in
both lists must hold for the term to match. Multiple terms inside
a NodeSelector are OR-combined, so the typical pattern is "many
small terms = OR alternatives" while "one term with many entries
= AND constraints".

matchExpressions.description: A list of node selector requirements by node's labels.

matchFields.description: A list of node selector requirements by node's fields.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Mixing what should be OR alternatives into a single term's `matchExpressions` and ending up with a term that can never match (e.g. `zone In [a]` AND `zone In [b]`).
Using `matchFields` for label data — `matchFields` targets API field paths, not labels; the lookup silently returns empty.
Empty matchExpressions AND empty matchFields — the term has no requirements and is treated as 'match all', which is rarely the intent inside a hard rule.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.NodeSelectorTerm.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use as elements of `nodeSelectorTerms` or inside
`preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution[].preference`.
Prefer multiple terms (OR semantics) over inflating one term with
conditional logic — the OR-of-ANDs shape is the standard mental
model for node selection.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.ObjectFieldSelector.description:
Downward API selector that exposes a single Pod field. `fieldPath`
is the dotted JSONPath into the Pod object — common values:
`metadata.name`, `metadata.namespace`, `metadata.uid`,
`metadata.labels['<key>']`, `metadata.annotations['<key>']`,
`spec.nodeName`, `spec.serviceAccountName`. Status fields like
`status.podIP`, `status.hostIP`, `status.podIPs[0].ip` work *only*
as env-var downward API (`valueFrom.fieldRef`), not in volume
projections. `apiVersion` is optional and defaults to `v1`.

apiVersion.description: Version of the schema the FieldPath is written in terms of, defaults to "v1".

fieldPath.description: Path of the field to select in the specified API version.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using `status.podIP` in a volume projection — only valid in env vars; the volume rejects it at admission.
Quoting paths incorrectly for keyed fields — `metadata.labels['app.kubernetes.io/name']` is correct; `metadata.labels.app.kubernetes.io/name` is not.
Reading the IP before Pod IP is assigned — when the env var is consumed at container init, `status.podIP` is already set, but very early Pod hooks may see empty.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.ObjectFieldSelector.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use to read Pod identity into env vars or files without granting
API server access. The chart auto-injects `POD_NAME`, `POD_NAMESPACE`,
`POD_IP`, `NODE_NAME` env vars by default — write this manually
only when you need a field the chart doesn't auto-expose, or a
different env-var name.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.PersistentVolumeClaim.description:
Desired state of a PersistentVolumeClaim. `accessModes` is the list of
mount modes the bound PV must support (`ReadWriteOnce`,
`ReadOnlyMany`, `ReadWriteMany`, `ReadWriteOncePod`); `resources`
is a VolumeResourceRequirements with `requests.storage` setting the
minimum size; `storageClassName` picks the StorageClass for dynamic
provisioning (omit for the cluster default, `""` explicit for static
binding only); `volumeMode` is `Filesystem` (default) or `Block`;
`dataSource`/`dataSourceRef` clones from a snapshot or PVC. After
creation, most fields are immutable — `resources.requests.storage`
is the only one that can be grown (and only if the StorageClass has
`allowVolumeExpansion: true`).

accessModes.description: accessModes contains the desired access modes the volume should have. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/persistent-volumes#access-modes-1

dataSource.description: dataSource field can be used to specify either: * An existing VolumeSnapshot object (snapshot.storage.k8s.io/VolumeSnapshot) * An existing PVC (PersistentVolumeClaim) If the provisioner or an external controller can support the specified data source, it will create a new volume based on the contents of the specified data source. When the AnyVolumeDataSource feature gate is enabled, dataSource contents will be copied to dataSourceRef, and dataSourceRef contents will be copied to dataSource when dataSourceRef.namespace is not specified. If the namespace is specified, then dataSourceRef will not be copied to dataSource.

dataSourceRef.description:
dataSourceRef specifies the object from which to populate the volume with data, if a non-empty volume is desired. This may be any object from a non-empty API group (non core object) or a PersistentVolumeClaim object. When this field is specified, volume binding will only succeed if the type of the specified object matches some installed volume populator or dynamic provisioner. This field will replace the functionality of the dataSource field and as such if both fields are non-empty, they must have the same value. For backwards compatibility, when namespace isn't specified in dataSourceRef, both fields (dataSource and dataSourceRef) will be set to the same value automatically if one of them is empty and the other is non-empty. When namespace is specified in dataSourceRef, dataSource isn't set to the same value and must be empty. There are three important differences between dataSource and dataSourceRef: * While dataSource only allows two specific types of objects, dataSourceRef
  allows any non-core object, as well as PersistentVolumeClaim objects.
* While dataSource ignores disallowed values (dropping them), dataSourceRef
  preserves all values, and generates an error if a disallowed value is
  specified.
* While dataSource only allows local objects, dataSourceRef allows objects
  in any namespaces.
(Beta) Using this field requires the AnyVolumeDataSource feature gate to be enabled. (Alpha) Using the namespace field of dataSourceRef requires the CrossNamespaceVolumeDataSource feature gate to be enabled.

resources.description: resources represents the minimum resources the volume should have. Users are allowed to specify resource requirements that are lower than previous value but must still be higher than capacity recorded in the status field of the claim. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/persistent-volumes#resources

selector.description: selector is a label query over volumes to consider for binding.

storageClassName.description: storageClassName is the name of the StorageClass required by the claim. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/persistent-volumes#class-1

volumeAttributesClassName.description: volumeAttributesClassName may be used to set the VolumeAttributesClass used by this claim. If specified, the CSI driver will create or update the volume with the attributes defined in the corresponding VolumeAttributesClass. This has a different purpose than storageClassName, it can be changed after the claim is created. An empty string or nil value indicates that no VolumeAttributesClass will be applied to the claim. If the claim enters an Infeasible error state, this field can be reset to its previous value (including nil) to cancel the modification. If the resource referred to by volumeAttributesClass does not exist, this PersistentVolumeClaim will be set to a Pending state, as reflected by the modifyVolumeStatus field, until such as a resource exists. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volume-attributes-classes/

volumeMode.description: volumeMode defines what type of volume is required by the claim. Value of Filesystem is implied when not included in claim spec.

volumeName.description: volumeName is the binding reference to the PersistentVolume backing this claim.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `storageClassName: ""` (empty string) intending to use the cluster default — `""` explicitly means 'no class, static binding only'; omit the field entirely for default.
Specifying `accessModes: [ReadWriteMany]` on a provider that doesn't support it (most block-storage providers don't) — the PVC stays Pending forever.
Trying to shrink storage by re-applying with a smaller `requests.storage` — shrinking is not supported; the apply is rejected or silently ignored depending on driver.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.PersistentVolumeClaim.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use as the `spec` of a top-level PVC or inside an
`EphemeralVolumeSource.volumeClaimTemplate`. Always specify
`accessModes` (no sensible default) and `resources.requests.storage`
(required). Omit `storageClassName` to use the cluster default;
set it explicitly for tiered storage (e.g. `gp3` vs `io2`).

k8s.io.api.core.v1.PersistentVolumeClaim.description:
Embedded PVC template used inside `EphemeralVolumeSource` to
materialize a per-Pod PVC bound to the Pod's lifecycle. Two fields:
`metadata` (labels and annotations applied to the generated PVC —
`name` is server-set as `<pod-name>-<volume-name>` and any name in
the template is rejected) and `spec` (a PersistentVolumeClaimSpec
describing the storage to provision). The kubelet creates the PVC
when the Pod is admitted and deletes it when the Pod is deleted —
it does NOT survive Pod rescheduling or Deployment rollouts.

metadata.description: May contain labels and annotations that will be copied into the PVC when creating it. No other fields are allowed and will be rejected during validation.

spec.description: The specification for the PersistentVolumeClaim. The entire content is copied unchanged into the PVC that gets created from this template. The same fields as in a PersistentVolumeClaim are also valid here.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `metadata.name` in the template — Kubernetes rejects the Pod; the name is server-generated.
Forgetting that the generated PVC is deleted with the Pod — Deployment rollouts kill old Pods which destroys their ephemeral PVCs, so data does not survive rollouts.
Requesting `ReadWriteMany` in a single-pod ephemeral template — supported but pointless; only one Pod ever attaches an ephemeral PVC, so RWO is correct.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.PersistentVolumeClaim.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use only inside `EphemeralVolumeSource.volumeClaimTemplate` — that
is its sole valid home. For shared durable storage, use a top-level
PVC and reference it via PersistentVolumeClaimVolumeSource. For
StatefulSet-style per-pod-stable PVCs, use the StatefulSet's
`volumeClaimTemplates` (different API shape, similar concept).

k8s.io.api.core.v1.PersistentVolumeClaim.description:
Mounts an existing PersistentVolumeClaim by name. `claimName`
references a PVC in the same namespace as the Pod; the PVC's bound
PersistentVolume is what actually mounts. `readOnly: true` forces a
read-only mount even if the PVC's access mode would allow writes
(rare — typically controlled via `volumeMounts[].readOnly`). The
PVC's `accessModes` determine whether multiple Pods can attach
concurrently — `ReadWriteOnce` means one node at a time (multiple
Pods on the same node may share), `ReadWriteMany` means multiple
nodes, `ReadOnlyMany` means multiple read-only mounts. Some
providers also support `ReadWriteOncePod` (Kubernetes 1.27+) for
strict single-Pod attachment.

claimName.description: claimName is the name of a PersistentVolumeClaim in the same namespace as the pod using this volume. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/persistent-volumes#persistentvolumeclaims

readOnly.description: readOnly Will force the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts. Default false.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Pointing `claimName` at a PVC in another namespace — silently fails; PVCs are namespace-local.
Attaching a `ReadWriteOnce` PVC to a multi-replica Deployment without per-replica affinity — every replica fights for the same volume, only one starts, the rest are Pending.
Setting `readOnly: true` here while the PVC's `accessModes` doesn't include `ReadOnlyMany` and the workload expects write access — the mount succeeds read-only and writes fail at runtime with EROFS.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.PersistentVolumeClaim.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for durable storage that must survive Pod restarts or
rescheduling. The chart's `persistentVolumeClaims:` top-level field
lets you declare PVCs alongside the Deployment; this volume source
then references them by name. For per-Pod ephemeral PVCs (created
and destroyed with the Pod), use an `EphemeralVolumeSource` instead.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.PhotonPersistentDiskV.description:
Volume backed by a VMware Photon Controller persistent disk. Legacy
vendor-specific in-tree driver retained for compatibility.

fsType.description: fsType is the filesystem type to mount. Must be a filesystem type supported by the host operating system. Ex. "ext4", "xfs", "ntfs". Implicitly inferred to be "ext4" if unspecified.

pdID.description: pdID is the ID that identifies Photon Controller persistent disk

k8s.io.api.core.v1.PodAffinity.description:
Co-location rules — schedule this Pod *near* other Pods matching a
label selector inside the same topology domain. Two flavors:
`requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` (hard — Pending if
no matching domain exists) and
`preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` (soft, weighted
preference). PodAffinity is expensive at scale: the scheduler
enumerates every Pod in the cluster (or in `namespaces`) to evaluate
the selector, which becomes a bottleneck above a few thousand pods.
Use `topologySpreadConstraints` for general spreading and reserve
PodAffinity for cases that genuinely need co-location (cache locality
with a specific peer service).

preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringEx.description: The scheduler will prefer to schedule pods to nodes that satisfy the affinity expressions specified by this field, but it may choose a node that violates one or more of the expressions. The node that is most preferred is the one with the greatest sum of weights, i.e. for each node that meets all of the scheduling requirements (resource request, requiredDuringScheduling affinity expressions, etc.), compute a sum by iterating through the elements of this field and adding "weight" to the sum if the node has pods which matches the corresponding podAffinityTerm; the node(s) with the highest sum are the most preferred.

requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExe.description: If the affinity requirements specified by this field are not met at scheduling time, the pod will not be scheduled onto the node. If the affinity requirements specified by this field cease to be met at some point during pod execution (e.g. due to a pod label update), the system may or may not try to eventually evict the pod from its node. When there are multiple elements, the lists of nodes corresponding to each podAffinityTerm are intersected, i.e. all terms must be satisfied.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Forgetting `topologyKey` — it has no default and the scheduler rejects the Pod at admission.
Using `required...` PodAffinity in a multi-zone cluster while the target service runs in a single zone — pods become Pending in other zones with no recovery.
Setting PodAffinity at large scale and seeing scheduler latency spike — the selector evaluates against every Pod in scope; constrain via `namespaces` or `namespaceSelector` or move to topologySpread.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.PodAffinity.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use only when co-location is a real performance win — e.g. an app
Pod that benefits from same-host caching alongside a cache Pod.
For "spread my pods evenly" prefer `topologySpreadConstraints`;
for "don't put two of my pods on the same host" use `podAntiAffinity`.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.PodAffinityTerm.description:
A single pod-affinity / pod-anti-affinity rule. `labelSelector` picks
target Pods; `topologyKey` is the node label that defines the
topology domain (`kubernetes.io/hostname` for "same node",
`topology.kubernetes.io/zone` for "same zone"); `namespaces` /
`namespaceSelector` scope which namespaces the selector evaluates
against (default: this Pod's namespace only). `matchLabelKeys` /
`mismatchLabelKeys` (Kubernetes 1.29+) compute per-Pod label values
and AND them into the selector — useful for "co-locate with Pods of
the same revision" without templating the label values.
