Your application will need to check for this condition, and re-read until they are consistent.

The named signer controls chooses the format of the certificate it issues; consult the signer implementation's documentation to learn how to use the certificates it issues.

secret.description: secret information about the secret data to project

serviceAccountToken.description: serviceAccountToken is information about the serviceAccountToken data to project

x-agent-common-mistakes: Populating two source slots in one VolumeProjection — the API rejects this; split into two separate entries in `sources[]`.
Forgetting that all sources share the same target mount path — collisions in `items[].path` cause silent overwrites.
Using VolumeProjection as a top-level volume — it must live inside `projected.sources[]`, not directly under the Pod's `volumes[]`.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.VolumeProjection.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use as the elements of `projected.sources[]`. Pick exactly one of
the source slots per entry; populating two is rejected. For a
single-source mount the standalone volume types (ConfigMapVolumeSource
etc.) are simpler than wrapping in a projected volume.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.VolumeResourceRequire.description:
Storage capacity request/limit for a PVC. `requests.storage` is the
minimum capacity the PV must satisfy — Kubernetes binds the smallest
matching PV that meets or exceeds the request. `limits.storage` is
rarely useful and mostly ignored by drivers. Values use Quantity
syntax (`100Mi`, `10Gi`, `1Ti`). After binding,
`requests.storage` is the only field that can be increased — and
only when the StorageClass has `allowVolumeExpansion: true`;
shrinking is never supported.

limits.description: Limits describes the maximum amount of compute resources allowed. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/manage-resources-containers/

requests.description: Requests describes the minimum amount of compute resources required. If Requests is omitted for a container, it defaults to Limits if that is explicitly specified, otherwise to an implementation-defined value. Requests cannot exceed Limits. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/manage-resources-containers/

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using lowercase suffixes — Kubernetes Quantity requires case-sensitive units (`Gi` not `gi`, `Mi` not `mi`).
Setting `requests.storage` below what the StorageClass minimums allow — many cloud-provider classes round up to a minimum (e.g. 1 GiB for AWS gp3); the bound PV may exceed the request.
Trying to shrink — applies are silently rejected by most drivers; the only way to shrink is to back up, recreate the PVC, and restore.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.VolumeResourceRequire.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use inside PersistentVolumeClaimSpec to size the volume. Pick the
request based on actual data + headroom; if the underlying class
supports expansion, you can grow later without downtime on most
drivers.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.VsphereVirtualDiskVol.description:
Volume backed by a vSphere VMDK. Legacy in-tree driver; for new
workloads prefer the csi.vsphere.vmware.com CSI driver.

fsType.description: fsType is filesystem type to mount. Must be a filesystem type supported by the host operating system. Ex. "ext4", "xfs", "ntfs". Implicitly inferred to be "ext4" if unspecified.

storagePolicyID.description: storagePolicyID is the storage Policy Based Management (SPBM) profile ID associated with the StoragePolicyName.

storagePolicyName.description: storagePolicyName is the storage Policy Based Management (SPBM) profile name.

volumePath.description: volumePath is the path that identifies vSphere volume vmdk

k8s.io.api.core.v1.WeightedPodAffinityTe.description:
Soft pod-affinity / pod-anti-affinity entry — pairs a `weight` (1–100)
with a `podAffinityTerm` (one PodAffinityTerm). Used inside
`preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution[]` for both
PodAffinity and PodAntiAffinity. The scheduler ranks candidate nodes
by summing weights of matching terms, choosing the highest score.
The chart's `autoCreateSoftAntiAffinity` emits a `weight: 100` entry
with the workload's own labels and `topologyKey:
kubernetes.io/hostname` — that's the typical shape for soft
hostname spread.

podAffinityTerm.description: Required. A pod affinity term, associated with the corresponding weight.

weight.description: weight associated with matching the corresponding podAffinityTerm, in the range 1-100.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `weight: 0` — rejected by the API; weight must be 1–100.

Pairing soft anti-affinity with a `topologyKey` that doesn't match real cluster topology (e.g. a label only the cloud provider sets but the nodes lack) — the scheduler treats every node as a distinct domain, defeating the spread intent.
Manually adding this on top of the chart's `autoCreateSoftAntiAffinity` injection — both apply and you may double-count weight, biasing harder than you intended.
k8s.io.api.core.v1.WeightedPodAffinityTe.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use to bias placement without forcing it — pods still schedule when
the preferred placement isn't available. For a hard guarantee use
`required...` with a plain PodAffinityTerm. For evenly-spread
replicas across zones/hosts, `topologySpreadConstraints` is more
predictable.

k8s.io.api.core.v1.WindowsSecurityContex.description:
Windows-specific security context — gMSA credential spec, runAsUserName,
and hostProcess flag for Pods running on Windows nodes.

gmsaCredentialSpec.description: GMSACredentialSpec is where the GMSA admission webhook (https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/windows-gmsa) inlines the contents of the GMSA credential spec named by the GMSACredentialSpecName field.

gmsaCredentialSpecName.description: GMSACredentialSpecName is the name of the GMSA credential spec to use.

hostProcess.description: HostProcess determines if a container should be run as a 'Host Process' container. All of a Pod's containers must have the same effective HostProcess value (it is not allowed to have a mix of HostProcess containers and non-HostProcess containers). In addition, if HostProcess is true then HostNetwork must also be set to true.

runAsUserName.description: The UserName in Windows to run the entrypoint of the container process. Defaults to the user specified in image metadata if unspecified. May also be set in PodSecurityContext. If set in both SecurityContext and PodSecurityContext, the value specified in SecurityContext takes precedence.

k8s.io.api.networking.v1.IPBlock.description: IPBlock describes a particular CIDR (Ex. "192.168.1.0/24","2001:db8::/64") that is allowed to the pods matched by a NetworkPolicySpec's podSelector. The except entry describes CIDRs that should not be included within this rule.

cidr.description: cidr is a string representing the IPBlock Valid examples are "192.168.1.0/24" or "2001:db8::/64"

except.description: except is a slice of CIDRs that should not be included within an IPBlock Valid examples are "192.168.1.0/24" or "2001:db8::/64" Except values will be rejected if they are outside the cidr range

k8s.io.api.networking.v1.NetworkPolicyEg.description: NetworkPolicyEgressRule describes a particular set of traffic that is allowed out of pods matched by a NetworkPolicySpec's podSelector. The traffic must match both ports and to. This type is beta-level in 1.8

ports.description: ports is a list of destination ports for outgoing traffic. Each item in this list is combined using a logical OR. If this field is empty or missing, this rule matches all ports (traffic not restricted by port). If this field is present and contains at least one item, then this rule allows traffic only if the traffic matches at least one port in the list.

to.description: to is a list of destinations for outgoing traffic of pods selected for this rule. Items in this list are combined using a logical OR operation. If this field is empty or missing, this rule matches all destinations (traffic not restricted by destination). If this field is present and contains at least one item, this rule allows traffic only if the traffic matches at least one item in the to list.

k8s.io.api.networking.v1.NetworkPolicyIn.description: NetworkPolicyIngressRule describes a particular set of traffic that is allowed to the pods matched by a NetworkPolicySpec's podSelector. The traffic must match both ports and from.

from.description: from is a list of sources which should be able to access the pods selected for this rule. Items in this list are combined using a logical OR operation. If this field is empty or missing, this rule matches all sources (traffic not restricted by source). If this field is present and contains at least one item, this rule allows traffic only if the traffic matches at least one item in the from list.

ports.description: ports is a list of ports which should be made accessible on the pods selected for this rule. Each item in this list is combined using a logical OR. If this field is empty or missing, this rule matches all ports (traffic not restricted by port). If this field is present and contains at least one item, then this rule allows traffic only if the traffic matches at least one port in the list.

k8s.io.api.networking.v1.NetworkPolicyPe.description: NetworkPolicyPeer describes a peer to allow traffic to/from. Only certain combinations of fields are allowed

ipBlock.description: ipBlock defines policy on a particular IPBlock. If this field is set then neither of the other fields can be.

namespaceSelector.description:
namespaceSelector selects namespaces using cluster-scoped labels. This field follows standard label selector semantics; if present but empty, it selects all namespaces.

If podSelector is also set, then the NetworkPolicyPeer as a whole selects the pods matching podSelector in the namespaces selected by namespaceSelector. Otherwise it selects all pods in the namespaces selected by namespaceSelector.

podSelector.description:
podSelector is a label selector which selects pods. This field follows standard label selector semantics; if present but empty, it selects all pods.

If namespaceSelector is also set, then the NetworkPolicyPeer as a whole selects the pods matching podSelector in the Namespaces selected by NamespaceSelector. Otherwise it selects the pods matching podSelector in the policy's own namespace.

k8s.io.api.networking.v1.NetworkPolicyPo.description: NetworkPolicyPort describes a port to allow traffic on

endPort.description: endPort indicates that the range of ports from port to endPort if set, inclusive, should be allowed by the policy. This field cannot be defined if the port field is not defined or if the port field is defined as a named (string) port. The endPort must be equal or greater than port.

port.description: port represents the port on the given protocol. This can either be a numerical or named port on a pod. If this field is not provided, this matches all port names and numbers. If present, only traffic on the specified protocol AND port will be matched.

protocol.description: protocol represents the protocol (TCP, UDP, or SCTP) which traffic must match. If not specified, this field defaults to TCP.

k8s.io.api.networking.v1.NetworkPolicySp.description: NetworkPolicySpec provides the specification of a NetworkPolicy

egress.description: egress is a list of egress rules to be applied to the selected pods. Outgoing traffic is allowed if there are no NetworkPolicies selecting the pod (and cluster policy otherwise allows the traffic), OR if the traffic matches at least one egress rule across all of the NetworkPolicy objects whose podSelector matches the pod. If this field is empty then this NetworkPolicy limits all outgoing traffic (and serves solely to ensure that the pods it selects are isolated by default). This field is beta-level in 1.8

ingress.description: ingress is a list of ingress rules to be applied to the selected pods. Traffic is allowed to a pod if there are no NetworkPolicies selecting the pod (and cluster policy otherwise allows the traffic), OR if the traffic source is the pod's local node, OR if the traffic matches at least one ingress rule across all of the NetworkPolicy objects whose podSelector matches the pod. If this field is empty then this NetworkPolicy does not allow any traffic (and serves solely to ensure that the pods it selects are isolated by default)

podSelector.description: podSelector selects the pods to which this NetworkPolicy object applies. The array of rules is applied to any pods selected by this field. An empty selector matches all pods in the policy's namespace. Multiple network policies can select the same set of pods. In this case, the ingress rules for each are combined additively. This field is optional. If it is not specified, it defaults to an empty selector.

policyTypes.description: policyTypes is a list of rule types that the NetworkPolicy relates to. Valid options are ["Ingress"], ["Egress"], or ["Ingress", "Egress"]. If this field is not specified, it will default based on the existence of ingress or egress rules; policies that contain an egress section are assumed to affect egress, and all policies (whether or not they contain an ingress section) are assumed to affect ingress. If you want to write an egress-only policy, you must explicitly specify policyTypes [ "Egress" ]. Likewise, if you want to write a policy that specifies that no egress is allowed, you must specify a policyTypes value that include "Egress" (since such a policy would not include an egress section and would otherwise default to just [ "Ingress" ]). This field is beta-level in 1.8

k8s.io.api.rbac.v1.PolicyRule.description: PolicyRule holds information that describes a policy rule, but does not contain information about who the rule applies to or which namespace the rule applies to.

apiGroups.description: APIGroups is the name of the APIGroup that contains the resources.  If multiple API groups are specified, any action requested against one of the enumerated resources in any API group will be allowed. "" represents the core API group and "*" represents all API groups.

nonResourceURLs.description: NonResourceURLs is a set of partial urls that a user should have access to.  *s are allowed, but only as the full, final step in the path Since non-resource URLs are not namespaced, this field is only applicable for ClusterRoles referenced from a ClusterRoleBinding. Rules can either apply to API resources (such as "pods" or "secrets") or non-resource URL paths (such as "/api"),  but not both.

resourceNames.description: ResourceNames is an optional white list of names that the rule applies to.  An empty set means that everything is allowed.

resources.description: Resources is a list of resources this rule applies to. '*' represents all resources.

verbs.description: Verbs is a list of Verbs that apply to ALL the ResourceKinds contained in this rule. '*' represents all verbs.

k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Fie.description:
FieldsV1 stores a set of fields in a data structure like a Trie, in JSON format.

Each key is either a '.' representing the field itself, and will always map to an empty set, or a string representing a sub-field or item. The string will follow one of these four formats: 'f:<name>', where <name> is the name of a field in a struct, or key in a map 'v:<value>', where <value> is the exact json formatted value of a list item 'i:<index>', where <index> is position of a item in a list 'k:<keys>', where <keys> is a map of  a list item's key fields to their unique values If a key maps to an empty Fields value, the field that key represents is part of the set.

The exact format is defined in sigs.k8s.io/structured-merge-diff

k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Lab.description:
Label-based selector with two forms that AND together: `matchLabels`
is a `{key: value}` map (every entry must match), `matchExpressions`
is a list of LabelSelectorRequirement entries (set-based `In`/`NotIn`
with values, presence-based `Exists`/`DoesNotExist`). An empty
LabelSelector (no matchLabels and no matchExpressions) matches *all*
objects in scope — different from `null`, which matches none. This
shape is reused everywhere a Kubernetes resource picks Pods,
ConfigMaps, Services, or other API objects: Deployment `selector`,
Service `selector`, ServiceMonitor `selector`, NetworkPolicy
`podSelector`, and PodAffinity rules.

matchExpressions.description: matchExpressions is a list of label selector requirements. The requirements are ANDed.

matchLabels.description: matchLabels is a map of {key,value} pairs. A single {key,value} in the matchLabels map is equivalent to an element of matchExpressions, whose key field is "key", the operator is "In", and the values array contains only "value". The requirements are ANDed.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting an empty LabelSelector (`{}`) and being surprised by overly broad matches — NetworkPolicy with `podSelector: {}` targets every Pod in the namespace, ServiceMonitor with `selector: {}` scrapes every Service.
Changing Deployment `selector.matchLabels` on an existing Deployment — the field is immutable; the apply fails with a clear error, but the fix is to delete and recreate, which is disruptive.
Forgetting that selectors are case-sensitive — `App: foo` and `app: foo` are different keys.
k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Lab.x-agent-when-to-use:
Prefer `matchLabels` for simple equality (most selectors). Use
`matchExpressions` when you need set operations (`In` with multiple
values, `NotIn`, presence checks). Be careful with the empty case:
`selector: {}` matches everything in scope; either populate it or
omit the field entirely.

k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Lab.description:
One set-based selector entry. `key` is the label name, `operator`
is `In`, `NotIn`, `Exists`, or `DoesNotExist`, and `values` is a
list whose meaning depends on the operator. With `In`/`NotIn`,
`values` must be non-empty; with `Exists`/`DoesNotExist`, `values`
must be empty. Multiple LabelSelectorRequirement entries inside a
LabelSelector's `matchExpressions` AND together — and they also AND
with `matchLabels`.

key.description: key is the label key that the selector applies to.

operator.description: operator represents a key's relationship to a set of values. Valid operators are In, NotIn, Exists and DoesNotExist.

values.description: values is an array of string values. If the operator is In or NotIn, the values array must be non-empty. If the operator is Exists or DoesNotExist, the values array must be empty. This array is replaced during a strategic merge patch.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Using `In` with an empty values list (or `Exists` with values) — the API validates this and rejects the parent resource.
Forgetting that `NotIn` matches objects that *also* have no value for the label — set-theoretic 'not in {x,y}' includes 'absent'; pair with `Exists` if you want 'has the label but not these values'.
Confusing this with NodeSelectorRequirement — the shape is the same but NodeSelectorRequirement also supports `Gt`/`Lt`; LabelSelectorRequirement does not.
k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Lab.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use inside `matchExpressions` when set semantics are needed: matching
one of several values (`In`), excluding a set (`NotIn`), or just
checking label presence (`Exists`/`DoesNotExist`). For plain `key:
value` equality, `matchLabels` is shorter and clearer.

k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Man.description:
Server-populated record of which manager (kubectl client, controller,
operator) owns which fields of an object. Driven by the server-side
apply mechanism: each apply submits a `fieldManager` name, and the
API server tracks ownership at field granularity in
`metadata.managedFields[]`. Users almost never write this directly —
it appears in API responses and matters mainly when debugging
"why is my controller fighting another controller for this field"
conflicts. The chart never sets this; it surfaces here only because
it appears in vendored ObjectMeta.

apiVersion.description: APIVersion defines the version of this resource that this field set applies to. The format is "group/version" just like the top-level APIVersion field. It is necessary to track the version of a field set because it cannot be automatically converted.

fieldsType.description: FieldsType is the discriminator for the different fields format and version. There is currently only one possible value: "FieldsV1"

fieldsV1.description: FieldsV1 holds the first JSON version format as described in the "FieldsV1" type.

manager.description: Manager is an identifier of the workflow managing these fields.

operation.description: Operation is the type of operation which lead to this ManagedFieldsEntry being created. The only valid values for this field are 'Apply' and 'Update'.

subresource.description: Subresource is the name of the subresource used to update that object, or empty string if the object was updated through the main resource. The value of this field is used to distinguish between managers, even if they share the same name. For example, a status update will be distinct from a regular update using the same manager name. Note that the APIVersion field is not related to the Subresource field and it always corresponds to the version of the main resource.

time.description: Time is the timestamp of when the ManagedFields entry was added. The timestamp will also be updated if a field is added, the manager changes any of the owned fields value or removes a field. The timestamp does not update when a field is removed from the entry because another manager took it over.

x-agent-common-mistakes: Writing this field in a manifest — the API server overwrites it; whatever you put gets discarded.
Two controllers using the same `fieldManager` name and fighting silently over a field — each apply takes ownership; pick distinct names for distinct actors.
Stripping `managedFields` from a GET response and reapplying without `--field-manager=<name>` — the resulting object's ownership map is wiped, which can produce surprises on the next apply.
k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Man.x-agent-when-to-use:
You generally do not write this; the API server populates it. When
debugging field-ownership conflicts, inspect this list to see which
`manager` owns the disputed field — that's the actor whose value is
preserved during apply. Use `kubectl apply --field-manager=<name>`
to set the manager name on your applies.

k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Obj.description:
The standard `metadata` block carried by every Kubernetes object.
`name` and `namespace` are identity; `labels` and `annotations` are
string maps with strict format rules (labels are filterable and
indexed, annotations are arbitrary metadata not used by selectors).
`ownerReferences`, `finalizers`, `managedFields`, `resourceVersion`,
`uid`, `generation`, `creationTimestamp`, and `deletionTimestamp`
are populated by the server. In this chart you generally do not
write ObjectMeta directly — the templates assemble it from
`metadata`, `labels`, `annotations`, `podLabels`, `podAnnotations`,
and chart-internal labels.

annotations.description: Annotations is an unstructured key value map stored with a resource that may be set by external tools to store and retrieve arbitrary metadata. They are not queryable and should be preserved when modifying objects. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/annotations

creationTimestamp.description:
CreationTimestamp is a timestamp representing the server time when this object was created. It is not guaranteed to be set in happens-before order across separate operations. Clients may not set this value. It is represented in RFC3339 form and is in UTC.

Populated by the system. Read-only. Null for lists. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#metadata

deletionGracePeriodSeconds.description: Number of seconds allowed for this object to gracefully terminate before it will be removed from the system. Only set when deletionTimestamp is also set. May only be shortened. Read-only.

deletionTimestamp.description:
DeletionTimestamp is RFC 3339 date and time at which this resource will be deleted. This field is set by the server when a graceful deletion is requested by the user, and is not directly settable by a client. The resource is expected to be deleted (no longer visible from resource lists, and not reachable by name) after the time in this field, once the finalizers list is empty. As long as the finalizers list contains items, deletion is blocked. Once the deletionTimestamp is set, this value may not be unset or be set further into the future, although it may be shortened or the resource may be deleted prior to this time. For example, a user may request that a pod is deleted in 30 seconds. The Kubelet will react by sending a graceful termination signal to the containers in the pod. After that 30 seconds, the Kubelet will send a hard termination signal (SIGKILL) to the container and after cleanup, remove the pod from the API. In the presence of network partitions, this object may still exist after this timestamp, until an administrator or automated process can determine the resource is fully terminated. If not set, graceful deletion of the object has not been requested.

Populated by the system when a graceful deletion is requested. Read-only. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#metadata

finalizers.description: Must be empty before the object is deleted from the registry. Each entry is an identifier for the responsible component that will remove the entry from the list. If the deletionTimestamp of the object is non-nil, entries in this list can only be removed. Finalizers may be processed and removed in any order.  Order is NOT enforced because it introduces significant risk of stuck finalizers. finalizers is a shared field, any actor with permission can reorder it. If the finalizer list is processed in order, then this can lead to a situation in which the component responsible for the first finalizer in the list is waiting for a signal (field value, external system, or other) produced by a component responsible for a finalizer later in the list, resulting in a deadlock. Without enforced ordering finalizers are free to order amongst themselves and are not vulnerable to ordering changes in the list.

generateName.description:
GenerateName is an optional prefix, used by the server, to generate a unique name ONLY IF the Name field has not been provided. If this field is used, the name returned to the client will be different than the name passed. This value will also be combined with a unique suffix. The provided value has the same validation rules as the Name field, and may be truncated by the length of the suffix required to make the value unique on the server.

If this field is specified and the generated name exists, the server will return a 409.

Applied only if Name is not specified. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#idempotency

generation.description: A sequence number representing a specific generation of the desired state. Populated by the system. Read-only.

labels.description: Map of string keys and values that can be used to organize and categorize (scope and select) objects. May match selectors of replication controllers and services. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/labels

managedFields.description: ManagedFields maps workflow-id and version to the set of fields that are managed by that workflow. This is mostly for internal housekeeping, and users typically shouldn't need to set or understand this field. A workflow can be the user's name, a controller's name, or the name of a specific apply path like "ci-cd". The set of fields is always in the version that the workflow used when modifying the object.

name.description: Name must be unique within a namespace. Is required when creating resources, although some resources may allow a client to request the generation of an appropriate name automatically. Name is primarily intended for creation idempotence and configuration definition. Cannot be updated. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names#names

namespace.description:
Namespace defines the space within which each name must be unique. An empty namespace is equivalent to the "default" namespace, but "default" is the canonical representation. Not all objects are required to be scoped to a namespace - the value of this field for those objects will be empty.

Must be a DNS_LABEL. Cannot be updated. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/namespaces

ownerReferences.description: List of objects depended by this object. If ALL objects in the list have been deleted, this object will be garbage collected. If this object is managed by a controller, then an entry in this list will point to this controller, with the controller field set to true. There cannot be more than one managing controller.

resourceVersion.description:
An opaque value that represents the internal version of this object that can be used by clients to determine when objects have changed. May be used for optimistic concurrency, change detection, and the watch operation on a resource or set of resources. Clients must treat these values as opaque and passed unmodified back to the server. They may only be valid for a particular resource or set of resources.

Populated by the system. Read-only. Value must be treated as opaque by clients and . More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#concurrency-control-and-consistency

selfLink.description: Deprecated: selfLink is a legacy read-only field that is no longer populated by the system.

uid.description:
UID is the unique in time and space value for this object. It is typically generated by the server on successful creation of a resource and is not allowed to change on PUT operations.

Populated by the system. Read-only. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names#uids

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `resourceVersion` in an apply manifest — the field is server-managed and an explicit value triggers conflict errors on every apply.
Putting selector-relevant data in `annotations` instead of `labels` — annotations are *not* matched by Service/Selector/NetworkPolicy selectors; only labels are.
Using a `name` longer than 63 characters (or violating DNS-1123 for namespaces/services) — the API rejects the object at admission with a non-obvious error path.
k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Obj.x-agent-when-to-use:
In this chart you set the *parts* (labels, annotations, name) via
typed fields; the full ObjectMeta is assembled for you. Direct use
matters when you're authoring raw manifests outside the chart, or
reading API responses to debug.

k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Own.description:
Server-populated link from a child object to a parent — drives
Kubernetes garbage collection. When all owners are deleted, the
child is garbage-collected (unless `blockOwnerDeletion: true` is set
on the reference, in which case the parent stays in Terminating
until the child is gone). `controller: true` marks one owner as the
*managing* controller — only one such entry per child; field
managers and HPA scale targets typically check it. The chart never
writes OwnerReferences; Kubernetes adds them automatically when a
controller (Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pod) creates child resources.

blockOwnerDeletion.description: If true, AND if the owner has the "foregroundDeletion" finalizer, then the owner cannot be deleted from the key-value store until this reference is removed. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/architecture/garbage-collection/#foreground-deletion for how the garbage collector interacts with this field and enforces the foreground deletion. Defaults to false. To set this field, a user needs "delete" permission of the owner, otherwise 422 (Unprocessable Entity) will be returned.

controller.description: If true, this reference points to the managing controller.

kind.description: Kind of the referent. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#types-kinds

name.description: Name of the referent. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names#names

uid.description: UID of the referent. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names#uids

x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting `controller: true` on multiple references — only one controller per object is allowed; the API rejects the second.
Forgetting `uid` — the field is required; without it the API rejects the reference. Pull the UID from the parent's `metadata.uid` at create time.
Cross-namespace ownerReferences — not supported; garbage collection only works within a single namespace (cluster-scoped owners are an exception). The reference is silently ignored.
k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Own.x-agent-when-to-use:
You rarely write this in manifests. When implementing a controller,
set owner references on objects you create so they're garbage-
collected when the parent CR is deleted. Use `controller: true` on
exactly one reference and `blockOwnerDeletion: true` if the parent's
finalization needs to wait for children.

k8s.io.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.Tim.description: Time is a wrapper around time.Time which supports correct marshaling to YAML and JSON.  Wrappers are provided for many of the factory methods that the time package offers.

configs.description:
ConfigMaps and Secrets managed by the chart. Each key becomes the
Kubernetes resource name (prefixed with the release name); type
selects ConfigMap vs Secret.
x-agent-common-mistakes: Putting binary content in data — base64 it into binaryData instead.
Forgetting type: secret — defaults to configMap, which won't be base64-encoded.
configs.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for in-chart ConfigMaps/Secrets that are mounted by chart
deployments. For externally-managed secrets, prefer the secretRefs
section with valueFrom references.

cronJobs.description: Map of CronJob resources keyed by name.

daemonSets.description:
Node-level agents that run one pod per matching node (log shippers,
monitoring agents, CNI plugins, storage drivers).
x-agent-common-mistakes: Setting replicas (DaemonSet pod count is managed by node count).
Setting hpa (DaemonSets cannot be horizontally autoscaled).
Forgetting tolerations for node taints (control-plane, GPU nodes).
Setting both updateStrategy.rollingUpdate.maxUnavailable=0 and maxSurge=0 — the rollout stalls; at least one must be > 0.
daemonSets.x-agent-example-use-case:
Fluent Bit log shipper with hostNetwork=true, tolerating all node
taints to run on every node.
daemonSets.x-agent-when-to-use:
Choose daemonSets when the workload must run on every node (or
every node matching a nodeSelector). For app workloads, prefer
deployments. For stateful per-pod identity, prefer statefulSets.

daemonSetsGeneral.description:
Chart-wide defaults applied to every daemonSets.* entry. Supports a
narrow subset of DaemonSetSpec: nodeSelector, tolerations, affinity,
securityContext, priorityClassName, probes. The narrowness is
intentional — DaemonSets do not have replicas, HPA, rollout strategy,
or migration concepts, so the general block mirrors only the
scheduling/security/probe fields that are universally applicable.
Per-instance values override these.

networkPolicy.description:
Default networkPolicy block for every daemonSets.* entry. Per-instance
values override this entirely. See docs/how-to/network-isolated-api.md
for merge semantics.

nodeSelector.description:
Default node label constraints for every DaemonSet pod
(passthrough). Per-instance nodeSelector overrides this entirely.

probes.description:
Default probes (liveness/readiness/startup) applied to
containers in every DaemonSet unless overridden.

tolerations.description:
Default pod tolerations applied to every DaemonSet (passthrough).
DaemonSets typically tolerate every taint so they land on every
node. Per-instance tolerations override this entirely.

items.description: One toleration (passthrough).

daemonSetsGeneral.x-agent-when-to-use:
Set platform-wide defaults here to avoid repeating boilerplate in
every daemonSets.<name>.

deployments.description:
Map of Deployment resources keyed by logical name. Each key becomes
the Kubernetes Deployment name (prefixed with the Helm release name).
Inherits defaults from deploymentsGeneral.
deployments.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use deployments for long-running stateless workloads. Each entry can
auto-create Service, Ingress, HPA, PDB, ServiceMonitor via flags.

deploymentsGeneral.description:
Global defaults applied to every entry under deployments. Same shape
as DeploymentSpec but without required fields. Per-deployment values
override the matching keys here (deep merge).
deploymentsGeneral.x-agent-when-to-use:
Set platform-wide defaults here to avoid repeating boilerplate in
every deployments.<name>.

generic.description:
Chart-wide settings shared by every resource: image pull secrets,
default service account, and per-resource-type defaults (ingressesGeneral,
httpRoutesGeneral, serviceMonitorGeneral).

annotations.description:
Default annotations merged into the metadata of every resource the
chart emits (Deployments, Services, Ingresses, etc.).

extraImagePullSecrets.description:
Image pull secret references added to every pod template the chart
renders. Use for private-registry credentials shared across all
workloads. Per-pod / per-ServiceAccount pull secrets stack on top.

labels.description:
Default labels merged into the metadata of every resource the chart
emits. Standard chart labels (app.kubernetes.io/*) are added on top.

generic.x-agent-when-to-use: Set platform-wide defaults that should not be repeated.

hpas.description:
Map of standalone HorizontalPodAutoscaler resources keyed by name.
Use this for HPAs that target resources not managed by this chart
(e.g. an externally-deployed Deployment).
hpas.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for HPAs not tied to a chart deployment. For chart deployments
prefer the inline hpa: block under deployments.<name>.

httpRoutes.description: Map of standalone HTTPRoute (Gateway API) resources keyed by name.
httpRoutes.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for traffic-splitting (canary), header-based routing, or any
Gateway API feature not covered by simple Ingress.

ingresses.description:
Map of standalone Ingress resources keyed by logical name. Use this for
Ingresses that span multiple services (e.g. one shared external Ingress
with paths routing to different deployments).
ingresses.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when one Ingress must route to multiple backends. For 1-deployment
ingresses, prefer autoCreateIngress on the deployment.

jobs.description:
Map of one-time Job resources keyed by name. Use cronJobs for scheduled
recurring runs.
jobs.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for one-off batch processing, data backfills, or one-shot setup
that runs as part of the chart install/upgrade.

persistentVolumeClaims.description:
Map of PersistentVolumeClaim resources keyed by name. Each entry creates
a PVC; deployments reference it through volumes[].persistentVolumeClaim.claimName.
persistentVolumeClaims.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use for stateful workloads or for shared scratch space across containers
within a pod.

additionalProperties.description:
One named group of env-var-to-Secret-key mappings. The map key
becomes the group name referenced by containers.<name>.secretRefs.
Each item is an env var (name) sourced from a Secret key.

secretRefs.description:
Map of reusable env-var groups keyed by name. Each group is a list of
env-var-to-Secret-key mappings. Containers reference a group by name
via containers.<name>.secretRefs: [groupName].
secretRefs.x-agent-when-to-use:
Use when the same Secret-derived env vars are needed by multiple
deployments or containers. Avoids repeating the same env mapping.

services.description:
Map of standalone Service resources keyed by logical name. For per-deployment
Services, prefer `autoCreateService: true` on a deployment.

statefulSets.description:
Workloads requiring stable network identity (DNS) and/or persistent
per-pod storage. Use for databases, queues, stateful caches.
x-agent-common-mistakes: Omitting serviceName (required by Kubernetes).
Using autoCreateService without headless mode (the chart defaults headless=true; only override if you have a specific reason).
Reusing a name shared with a deployments key (component labels collide).
Combining hpa with podManagementPolicy=OrderedReady — scale-down is sequential and slow, causing HPA oscillation. Prefer podManagementPolicy=Parallel when using HPA.
Omitting metadata.name on a volumeClaimTemplates entry — Kubernetes requires it (it becomes the volume name and the PVC name prefix).
statefulSets.x-agent-example-use-case:
Single-replica Postgres with one persistent volume and an autoCreated
headless Service for stable DNS.
statefulSets.x-agent-when-to-use:
Choose statefulSets when pods need stable hostnames, persistent
volumes that survive rescheduling, or strict ordering during
rolling updates. For stateless apps, prefer deployments.

statefulSetsGeneral.description:
Chart-wide defaults applied to every statefulSets.* entry. Shares
the scheduling/security/probe shape with deploymentsGeneral but
omits fields that do not apply to StatefulSets (strategy,
parallelism, completions, migrations, hpa, serviceType). Per-instance
values override.

networkPolicy.description:
Default networkPolicy block for every statefulSets.* entry. Per-instance
values override this entirely. See docs/how-to/network-isolated-api.md
for merge semantics.

nodeSelector.description:
Default node label constraints for every StatefulSet pod
(passthrough). Per-instance nodeSelector overrides this entirely.

probes.description:
Default probes (liveness/readiness/startup) applied to
containers in every StatefulSet unless overridden.

tolerations.description:
Default pod tolerations applied to every StatefulSet
(passthrough). Per-instance tolerations override this entirely.

items.description: One toleration (passthrough).

statefulSetsGeneral.x-agent-when-to-use:
Set platform-wide defaults here to avoid repeating boilerplate in
every statefulSets.<name>.
